The phrase “dapat may makulong” has turned from a call for justice into a daily reflex. This piece examines how even those in power now echo it, watching instead of acting. A look at what happens when accountability becomes a wish instead of a duty, a hope instead of an expectation.

There’s a phrase you’ll hear almost every day now — from the driver stuck in traffic, the office worker checking their feed during a break, or the parent shaking their head over breakfast: “Dapat may makulong.”

It sounds like a demand, but it isn’t. It’s a wish. A tired kind of hope that justice might still be a thing that happens in the Philippines, even if the rich and the powerful rarely face it. It’s what we mutter while reading about yet another scandal involving family names we’ve seen on ballots for fifty years, or another investigation that will take years — if it even makes it past the headlines.

When people say “dapat may makulong” today, they’re not expecting anyone to actually end up behind bars. They’re expressing the small faith that maybe this time, someone will. That maybe justice isn’t entirely a myth. But deep down, the phrase has become more prayer than prediction — the modern version of lighting a candle for a miracle that never comes.

## From Expectation to Wish

There was a time when “dapat may makulong” carried real weight — when people said it believing the law could still deliver justice. But over the years, that expectation slowly turned into a wish.

The phrase means “someone should be imprisoned.” Yet the way it’s said now reveals something else entirely: the erosion of trust in institutions meant to uphold the law. It’s no longer rooted in confidence that the system works. It’s rooted in the frustration of a people who already know accountability is unlikely.

The words haven’t changed, but the meaning has. What used to be an assertion of justice has become a sigh of collective exhaustion. It’s a vocalization of powerlessness and frustration — a reminder that in today’s Philippines, the law’s promise and its practice rarely meet.

## The Laws That Look Good on Paper

The Philippines doesn’t lack laws. The 1987 Constitution already says public office is a public trust. There’s the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act — lays out how to prosecute those who abuse it. There’s the Office of the Ombudsman, created specifically to investigate and charge corrupt officials, and the Sandiganbayan, a court built specifically to handle those cases.

On paper, it all looks solid. These laws were designed to ensure that anyone who misuses public funds or positions faces real consequences. But in the Philippines, the problem isn’t the framework — it’s the distance between what the law promises and what it actually delivers.

Take the 14 engineers from local governments and the Department of Public Works and Highways who’ve been named in various hearings — many of them linked to knowledge of infrastructure corruption. Investigations crawl while witnesses suddenly change their names. Some engineers even give sworn testimony exposing kickbacks worth billions, pointing directly to higher-ups. But years pass, and nothing meaningful happens.

Then there’s the 2025 flood control scandal. Senate probes uncovered over 400 ghost projects, padded budgets, and billions in “hidden” insertions designed to funnel public money to politicians and contractors. Witnesses described delivery trucks that never arrived and roads that exist only in reports. These projects were linked to senators, congressmen, and even former House speakers. The evidence is there. The witnesses have spoken. Yet, the same people continue working, some even retiring comfortably on government pensions.

The law exists. What’s missing is enforcement.

## The Officials Who Wish Alongside Us

What’s worse is that even the people in charge of enforcing the law sound like they’ve given up. The very officials who should be filing charges are the same ones demanding accountability are left hoping someone else will do it.

The Office of the Ombudsman barely has the funding to keep up with the scale of corruption it’s supposed to investigate. The Sandiganbayan is drowning in old cases — some dating back to the 1980s, including those tied to the Marcos and Duterte families, involving billions. Those cases didn’t fail because of due process. They failed because the system ran out of energy, or interest, or courage.

None of this is accidental. It’s what happens when positions meant for professionals are handed to people based on their surname. When every appointment is a political favor, not a decision made on competence. Departments stop communicating. Law enforcement agencies turn into silos. Corruption keeps breathing because the people who should suffocate it are the same ones keeping the doors open.

The Marcos administration naming Sara Duterte as Secretary of Education said it all. No experience, no background in the sector — just political exchange. Even former Cabinet members and lawmakers have said it outright: the President chose a partner, not a person who could understand governance. The country’s top offices are filled with people chosen for loyalty over skill, and in that kind of environment, accountability isn’t just rare — it’s impossible.

## The Collapse of Consequence

Everything looks intact on paper — the laws, the agencies, the offices that exist to enforce accountability. But a system only works when there are consequences. And in the Philippines, consequence has collapsed.

The legal system becomes a performance when no one faces real punishment. When public officials accused of stealing billions retire in peace, it sends a clear message: accountability is negotiable.

Each time a senator names another senator in a corruption hearing and nothing comes of it, it confirms what the public has long suspected — that justice is reserved for those without power. When the same political families win elections despite being tied to scandals, consequence becomes a story we stopped believing.

People adjust. They stop expecting the law to work and start using hope as a substitute for justice. That’s why “dapat may makulong” turns into a release valve — something to say when there’s nothing left to do.

Over time, that kind of resignation rewires how society sees the law. It stops being a guarantee and starts being a suggestion. It creates a vacuum where the system doesn’t serve the public anymore. It protects the few who know how to use it.

## Beyond Corruption: The Governance Vacuum

People keep saying corruption is the country’s biggest problem. It’s what every campaign promises to fix. But corruption is just one symptom of something deeper. The real problem is the way the government is run — or not run at all.

Every administration arrives with the same promise to end corruption. Every new president swears to clean the house. But the machinery underneath stays the same: slow, reactive, and badly managed. The Philippines doesn’t just need less corruption; it needs leaders who actually know how to govern.

Competence and integrity are not the same thing. A person can be honest and still incapable of managing a system. When leaders lack the ability to manage, they default to old habits: appointing friends, avoiding hard decisions, and allowing inefficiency. That’s where the country keeps getting stuck — between good intentions and bad execution.

When public officials lack the skill to make the system work, the gap is filled by those who know how to exploit it. Policies serve whoever’s in charge. Ordinary citizens end up paying for both the corruption and the incompetence. It’s a dual burden that makes it hard for anything to survive.

## The Resignation Behind the Prayer

At this point, “dapat may makulong” has stopped being an expression of outrage. It’s a sign of resignation. People say it because they’ve accepted that the system no longer works the way it should.

Surveys in 2025 showed that most Filipinos believe corruption is widespread. No one was surprised. They’ve heard the same promises from every administration. They’ve watched officials campaign on reform, win, and then keep the same structures that make reform impossible. They’ve seen projects vanish, funds misused, and cases dropped. The cycle repeats so often that expecting anything else feels pointless.

After decades of watching the same patterns, citizens stop expecting the law to protect them. They say “dapat may makulong” but deep down, they already know how it ends. Even the people inside government — the ones paid to make the system work — sound the same. They talk about fixing things but act like they’re waiting for someone else to do it.

That’s when it turns dangerous. Because once the people running the system start wishing for justice instead of enforcing it, the government stops being functional. It becomes a spectator to its own failures.

## What “Dapat May Makulong” Really Means Now

The phrase has changed meaning over time. It used to be about consequence. Now it’s more about belief. When people say “dapat may makulong,” they’re not talking about justice in a legal sense anymore. They’re talking about wanting to believe it still exists.

It’s no longer just a call for punishment. It’s a way of holding on to faith that the system might one day remember its purpose. It’s a reminder that wrongdoing should still matter, even when evidence says it doesn’t.

For many Filipinos, the phrase has become the smallest possible definition of justice. It doesn’t ask for systemic reform or equality on a national scale. It asks for one thing — that someone, anyone, finally faces consequence. It’s not much to ask for.

The tragedy is that even that minimum never comes.

## The Country That Hopes Instead of Acts

People keep saying “dapat may makulong” not because they expect justice, but because they’ve grown used to hoping for it. It’s a habit that has outlived belief, a quiet reflex that fills the space where trust in institutions used to be.

The phrase has turned into a way to cope — something people repeat to remind themselves that justice is still a concept, even if only in words. What keeps the system standing isn’t confidence in its laws or its courts, but the fact that the people keep wishing for fairness despite everything they’ve seen.

The structure survives because people keep it alive with hope. Each time someone says “dapat may makulong,” it creates the illusion that something still functions — that accountability hasn’t disappeared completely. But wishing isn’t the same as demanding, and expecting is easier than acting. As long as the cycle stops at hope, the rest never follows.

Maybe the phrase has become less about justice and more about endurance. It reflects how much faith has been lost and how much resignation people have learned to carry. We keep saying it because it feels like movement, because the alternative is admitting that justice, as we knew it, no longer exists.

Mornings are for writing. It’s when my head’s clear enough to put thoughts into words before the noise of the day sets in.

But lately, even before I start drafting, my Facebook feed looks like a landfill of recycled propaganda, recycled lines, the same DDS pages that never seem to die.

It’s strange because I had to start over. The original Morning Coffee Thoughts page got hacked, so I built a new one from scratch. Now the platform doesn’t seem to recognize me.

Since I often write about the garbage the Dutertes left behind, the system seems to think I’m one of their fans. It’s feeding me their content like it’s doing me a favor.

I’ve known for a while that there’s an unseen filter shaping what reaches us online. But this is the first time it’s felt personal. This blog takes a serious look at how our feeds are shaped—how they track, guess, and decide what we see—and what we can do to stop the system from promoting propaganda. A guide to taking back control, cleaning up your Facebook feed, and blocking the DDS.

Because every scroll, every pause, every click teaches it something about who we are. And the more I look at what it’s showing me, the more I realize: it’s not just the content that shapes people. The system shaping the content needs watching too.

## Understanding How Social Media Builds Your Feed

The feed you see isn’t random. It’s built piece by piece by a system that studies what catches your eye. Every scroll, every pause, every reaction feeds into that invisible process that decides what shows up next.

It begins with what’s called inventory — all the posts available to you. That includes updates from friends, pages you follow, groups you’ve joined, and even public posts you’ve never interacted with before ([SocialBee](https://socialbee.com/blog/facebook-algorithm/), [Hootsuite](https://blog.hootsuite.com/facebook-algorithm/)).

Then comes the signals phase. The platform looks at hundreds of data points: who posted it, what kind of content it is, when it was shared, and how you’ve interacted with similar posts in the past ([Planable](https://planable.io/blog/guide-to-social-media-algorithms/)).

After that, the system makes predictions. It tries to guess how you’ll behave — whether you’ll like, share, comment, or watch a video till the end. Each prediction helps it learn what to prioritize.

Finally, everything gets a relevance score. The higher the score, the more likely it appears at the top of your feed ([Buffer](https://buffer.com/resources/facebook-algorithm/)).

The cycle repeats every time you scroll. It’s fast, constant, and fine-tuned to keep you from leaving. Some argue these systems are neutral, but neutrality in this context is tricky. When engagement becomes the measure of value, whatever gets the most reactions ends up dominating the feed ([Knight Columbia](https://knightcolumbia.org/content/understanding-social-media-recommendation-algorithms)).

That’s why the same posts, faces, and political pages keep resurfacing, even when you’ve moved on. The system isn’t trying to understand you — it’s trying to keep you scrolling.

## How the System Tracks Your Behavior

The moment you open an app, it starts watching. Not in a paranoid way — just quietly, constantly, and very, very fast. Everything you do tells it something.

It begins with what you click, read, and watch. Each reaction — like, comment, share, or even just a few seconds of viewing — becomes a clue to your interests ([Tencent Cloud](https://www.tencentcloud.com/techpedia/119237), [Wired](https://www.wired.com/story/tiktok-data-privacy/)). On TikTok, for example, your viewing time, likes, follows, and even the kind of videos you linger on form a detailed behavioral profile ([Sage Journals](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/20563051221086241)).

Then comes dwell time — how long your eyes stay on a post before you move on. It’s one of the strongest signals that the system measures. Even without liking or commenting, just staring at something for a few seconds tells it to find more of the same ([The Social Content Factory](https://thesocialcontentfactory.com/blog/major-update-instagrams-2025-algorithm-prioritizes-dwell-time), [Prominence Global](https://www.prominence.global/linkedins-dwell-time-how-to-create-content-that-keeps-users-engaged), [arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.10464)).

It also pays attention to how you scroll. Every pause, flick, and hesitation becomes a datapoint. Slowing down on a certain post tells it to find more of the same. Speeding past something tells it to show less of that in the future ([Amplitude](https://amplitude.com/blog/3-metrics-modeling-mobile-user-behavior), [Rose & Gold](https://roseandgold.com/the-science-of-scrolling-understanding-user-behavior-on-social-platforms)).

The scariest part is that you don’t even have to engage for it to learn from you. Just looking counts. It’s a strange feeling — knowing your silence online is still being studied.

## The Technical Side: What Your Smartphone Sensors Reveal

Phones today do more than track what you click. They can also sense how you look, move, and react. This isn’t science fiction; it’s already part of how most modern devices work.

Some apps can estimate where your eyes are focused using just the front camera. Studies have shown that with the right hardware, your phone can follow your eye movement with surprising accuracy ([Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-18360-5), [PubMed Central](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8975177/)).

Then there’s facial expression analysis — systems that detect micro-expressions to guess what you’re feeling while scrolling. It’s not just theoretical anymore; researchers have mapped emotional responses in real time using facial data captured through the camera ([JMIR](https://www.jmir.org/2025/1/e68942), [UniBW](https://www.unibw.de/usable-security-and-privacy/publikationen/pdf/kosch2020avi.pdf)).

Every tap and swipe also leaves a trace. Touch behavior — the way you scroll, the rhythm of your typing, the pressure of your press — can reveal patterns unique to you ([PubMed Central](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6686626/), [Kostakos.org](http://kostakos.org/)).

And behind all that, your phone is quietly counting how long you spend on each screen. Some apps even track how often you tilt the page to calculate how long your attention stays in one place ([Medical Xpress](https://medicalxpress.com/news/2025-08-hour-day-social-media-scrolling.html)).

It’s easy to forget how much these small movements add up. Every second, your phone is building a picture of how you think, react, and linger. Not through what you say — but through what your body unconsciously gives away.

## The “Always Listening” Microphone: Eavesdropping and Ad Targeting

Phones today don’t just watch. They listen too — even when you’re not talking to them.

Voice assistants like “Hey Siri” or “OK Google” keep the microphone active so they can respond anytime. But it doesn’t stop when other apps also have microphone access. If you’ve granted permission, they can pick up snippets of your conversation even when the app isn’t open ([Norton](https://us.norton.com/blog/how-to/is-my-phone-listening-to-me), [Surfshark](https://surfshark.com/blog/is-my-phone-listening-to-me), [Spiralytics](https://www.spiralytics.com/blog/mobile-ads-can-phone-hear-conversations-infographic/)).

Ever notice how you mention a product in conversation, then suddenly see ads for it? That isn’t coincidence. Audio segments can be analyzed for keywords and linked to your profile for ad targeting. The recordings are often deleted, but the extracted data remains ([New Atlas](https://newatlas.com/computers/smartphone-listening-conversations-ads-facebook/)).

The fix is simple, though not automatic. Check which apps have microphone access. Deny the ones that don’t need it to function. Most people forget these settings exist — and that’s exactly why background listening goes unnoticed.

Once I reviewed mine, I realized dozens of apps I never use still had permission to hear me. It was a good time to clear them out and finally switch them off.

Our devices already collect so much. We can’t stop every form of tracking, but we can draw a line with the things that feel like they’re crossing it. What we say in the room through the mic should stay private.

## Platform Policies, Moderation, and Legal Remedies

There’s another layer to all this — one that decides which voices get amplified and which quietly fade away.

When a post or page is reported as spam or misinformation by enough people, Facebook and similar platforms often lower its visibility. It can be flagged, hidden from timelines, or placed under review without the user ever knowing why. These user reports, whether coordinated or not, often dictate what the public gets to see ([Internet Governance Forum](https://www.intgovforum.org/sites/default/files/webform/ssrn-id3792097.pdf)).

The process sounds fair on paper, but it isn’t always. Some groups use reporting tools to silence criticism or debate. I’ve seen people lose their pages overnight — not because they spread lies, but because their truths upset the wrong audience.

Still, there are real protections in place for those who experience digital harassment or defamation. In the Philippines, online abuse and organized misinformation campaigns can fall under cybercrime and privacy laws. Victims can report to the NBI or consult experts who specialize in online accountability ([Respicio.ph](http://respicio.ph/)).

Social media often feels lawless, but that’s not entirely true. The same tools used to manipulate your feed can also be used to defend it — if you know your rights and how to respond.

## Step-by-Step: Blocking and Filtering on Major Platforms

After learning how these systems read us, I started cleaning up my own feed. It’s not dramatic work, but these are small steps that make your space a little lighter each time.

### Facebook
*   Go to any post and tap the three dots. Choose **Hide post** or **Snooze [account]**.
*   To stop seeing a page altogether, open it and hit **Block**.
*   You can also fine-tune your feed under **Settings > News Feed Preferences**. From there, unfollow, snooze, or prioritize who you actually want to hear from. ([Facebook Help Center](https://www.facebook.com/help) via [SocialBee](https://socialbee.com/blog/facebook-algorithm/))

### Instagram
*   Tap the three dots on a post and select **Not Interested** or **Report**.
*   For persistent accounts, go to their profile, tap the three dots again, and hit **Block**. ([Instagram Support](https://help.instagram.com) via [Hootsuite](https://blog.hootsuite.com/facebook-algorithm/))

### TikTok
*   Long-press any video and choose **Not Interested** or **Report**.
*   If a user keeps showing up on your “For You” page, open their profile, tap the three dots, and select **Block**. ([TikTok Safety Center](https://www.tiktok.com/safety) via [Wired](https://www.wired.com/story/tiktok-data-privacy/))

Each platform updates these settings often, so it helps to check their help pages every few months. Small actions add up. Blocking one account doesn’t feel like much, but over time your feed begins to look like yours again.

## The Tolerance Trap: Why Scrolling Past Content Backfires

There was a time when I thought ignoring DDS content was enough. Scroll fast, don’t engage, and maybe it’ll stop showing up. I was wrong.

Every time you pause on a post, even for a heartbeat, the system interprets it as interest. It doesn’t care if you’re angry or amused—it only measures time and attention. That’s how hate pages keep slipping back into your feed.

Dwell time, scroll speed, and passive viewing all feed the same machine that curates your timeline. The more you watch, the stronger the signal that you “like” that kind of content ([Metricool](https://metricool.com/social-media-algorithms/), [Dash Social](https://www.dashsocial.com/blog/facebook-algorithm), [Brandwatch](https://www.brandwatch.com/blog/social-media-algorithm/)).

Blocking, muting, or marking posts as “Not Interested” sends the opposite signal. It teaches the system what you don’t want to see. It’s the difference between ignoring the noise and actually building a wall against it.

Arguing in the comments does the worst kind of damage—it tells the platform the post is engaging, making it more visible to others.

Silence, when it means scrolling past, isn’t neutrality. It’s fuel. And that’s how the algorithm keeps the garbage floating to the top.

## The Blocking Strategy: Taking Active Control

After learning how the system reacts to every move we make, the next step is simple but deliberate: active filtering.

*   Block, hide, or report any account or page that spreads propaganda or disinformation.
*   Unfollow and mute contacts whose posts drain your focus.
*   Use keyword filters when the option exists.
*   And most importantly, never comment, argue, or react to bait posts—each response tells the system to keep that content on your feed ([SocialBu](https://socialbu.com/blog/social-media-content-filtering)).

If you’re part of an online group or advocacy page, take it further. Encourage **mass blocking** and **mass reporting** when coordinated disinformation campaigns appear. These actions push the system to lower the visibility of those pages faster.

Reclaiming control isn’t a one-time act. It’s a habit. The more consistently you block and filter, the cleaner your timeline becomes. You’re not just cleaning your feed; you’re deciding what kind of conversation deserves to be seen.

## The Limits of User Control

Even with all the blocking, filtering, and cleaning up, there’s only so much we can control. These systems are built to keep us engaged, not to protect our peace.

DDS content can easily reappear under new names or recycled pages. Sometimes, even when a post gets reported, it stays through because it doesn’t technically break any platform rule. And then there are sponsored posts—ads that will find you whatever you tell the feed you don’t want to see ([SocialBu](https://socialbu.com/blog/social-media-content-filtering)).

Browser tools can help a little. Extensions that filter keywords or mute pages can reduce noise, though they don’t stop the tracking completely ([TechLockdown](https://www.techlockdown.com/guides/block-social-media)).

There’s no such thing as a perfectly clean timeline. Platforms change constantly, and what works today might be different tomorrow. But cleaning up isn’t just a technical act — it’s a form of self-defense.

When you start filtering what you let in, you’re not escaping reality. You’re choosing which parts of it you want to focus on.

## Privacy, Ethics, and Mindful Tech Use

At some point, it stops feeling like technology and starts feeling like intrusion. Every scroll, tap, or glance becomes another entry in a data file you’ll never see.

The truth is, constant tracking and background sensors raise real questions about consent. Many of us clicked “Accept” without reading the fine print, and now those quiet permissions have turned into a permanent spotlight. These data-hungry systems trade in what we read, what we buy, and how long we stare at a screen ([FS Poster](https://www.fs-poster.com/blog/how-social-media-sites-use-your-personal-data)).

Privacy laws exist, but enforcement is patchy. What’s legal in one country might be abuse in another. It’s why we have to review app permissions, and use browser tools that reduce tracking. The goal isn’t paranoia—it’s awareness.

There’s also the mental side of all this. Doom-scrolling through noise and hostility takes a toll. Studies consistently link heavy exposure to polarizing content with higher stress and anxiety ([PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11804976/), [Information Matters](https://informationmatters.org/2025/02/the-double-edged-scroll-active-vs-passive-social-media-use-and-stress/)). The mind wasn’t built to handle endless outrage.

Learning to scroll with intention matters. Curate your feed like you curate your surroundings. You are the only one who can decide what deserves your time and attention. The line between awareness and exhaustion is thin—don’t let an algorithm start mistaking noise for truth.

## Collective, National Action and Advocacy

Cleaning up your own feed helps, but the fight doesn’t end there. What we see online isn’t shaped by one person alone — it’s the product of millions of clicks feeding a machine.

When coordinated misinformation drives flood timelines, individual blocking isn’t enough. That’s where collective action matters. Educate friends, family, and coworkers about how engagement works. Encourage them to report troll pages and propaganda networks when they appear. When whole communities move in sync, the system starts to shift.

Schools can also play a part. Digital literacy should be as basic as reading and writing — teaching young Filipinos how to question what they see, check sources, and recognize manipulation. It’s not paranoia; it’s survival in an environment designed to capture attention, not tell the truth.

At a larger scale, we need stronger policies. Lawmakers and regulators should demand transparency about how these feeds are built and moderated. Platforms must be held accountable for how their systems shape public opinion and political discourse ([Knight Columbia](https://knightcolumbia.org/content/understanding-social-media-recommendation-algorithms)).

It’s time to treat digital misinformation the same way we treat pollution — something that doesn’t just exist online but spills over into real life, poisoning how people think, vote, and decide.

## Empower Yourself and Your Community

Awareness only matters if it turns into action. Each block, unfollow, or report teaches the system what kind of content doesn’t belong in your space.

Show others how to do it. Not through lectures or long debates, but through quiet example. When people see your feed free of trolls and noise, they’ll start asking how you did it — and that’s where the real teaching begins.

Be mindful of what you engage with. Every second you spend on a post, even in silence, tells the system to show you more of it. Guard your attention like something valuable. Protect it from the things designed to make you angry, tired, or numb.

We can’t rely on platforms to clean up the mess. Change begins when users stop rewarding the noise with attention.

So the next time a DDS post appears on your screen, don’t argue, don’t react, and don’t stay. Block it. Report it. Move on.

When enough people do this, the system learns a new pattern — one that favors awareness over outrage.

The internet reflects what we feed it. Let it mirror a country that values truth.

And yes, block the DDS.

There’s a common reaction when people hear about lawyers defending someone accused of a terrible crime. A question comes up again and again: “How can you defend someone like that?” It’s a question rooted in a deep misunderstanding, and human rights lawyer Kristina Conti believes it’s time we all got educated about what lawyers really do and who they actually serve.

Conti, who has spent more than a decade representing victims of drug war killings and fighting state abuses, has called for better public understanding of the true role of lawyers. Her message is simple but powerful: the legal profession must always serve justice and integrity, not just clients with money or popular causes.

## The Big Misunderstanding

Here’s what most people get wrong: they think lawyers are on the same side as their clients, morally and personally. We imagine that defending someone accused of a crime means you approve of them, or that representing different groups means you share their politics.

Nothing could be further from the truth.

When a lawyer defends someone in court, the point is to uphold principles much bigger than any single person. Every person deserves a fair trial, the government must properly prove any charge, and due process protects all. These are the foundations of democracy itself.

Think of it this way: if lawyers only defended people they personally believed were innocent, then lawyers would essentially be acting as judges before any trial happened. That’s not how justice works. The Constitution guarantees everyone the presumption of innocence until proven guilty, and lawyers make that guarantee real.

## Why Everyone Needs a Lawyer—Guilty or Not

Conti grew up watching her mother work as a public attorney, getting paid in bananas, fish, and tripe because her clients were poor. From an early age, she learned that being a lawyer wasn’t glamorous. It meant hard work, intelligence, and resourcefulness in service of people who had nothing else.

That experience shaped her understanding of what lawyers should be: defenders of the rule of law for everyone, especially those the system might otherwise crush.

Even people who actually committed crimes need lawyers. Why? Because the punishment must fit the crime. Without a lawyer, a person might be treated unfairly or given a sentence much harsher than the law allows. A lawyer can argue for appropriate sentencing while explaining the full context of someone’s life—their struggles, mental health struggles, or the circumstances that led them there. Without lawyers doing this work, the system could just steamroll people based on whatever the prosecutor demanded.

In 2019, a young jeepney driver in Manila was accused of theft after taking home a passenger’s lost bag. A public defender found evidence that the driver intended to return the bag and had even posted online looking for the owner. Instead of jail, the court allowed the driver to go free with a warning—because the lawyer dug for the truth and pressed for fairness. This is the kind of work many lawyers do, far from the media glare.

## People's Lawyers vs. Lawyers for the Powerful

Conti identifies as a “people’s lawyer,” which is different from your typical attorney. People’s lawyers, or pro-bono lawyers, represent marginalized groups—the poor, workers, farmers, activists, and others caught in massive power imbalances.

She didn’t stumble into this work. Conti started as a science student, then became a journalist at Bulatlat. The turning point came when a dear friend disappeared while doing community work. The government’s inaction on the case of missing activists pushed her to law school in 2008. She wanted to find her friend and help other victims of desparecidos (disappeared persons).

After passing the bar in 2014, she joined the National Union of Peoples’ Lawyers (NUPL), where she handled some of the most explosive cases: defending students falsely accused of weapons possession, pursuing justice for activists who had disappeared, and representing victims of Rodrigo Duterte’s drug war.

When thousands of mostly poor Filipinos were killed in the drug war, Conti knew someone had to take their cases. Many lawyers wouldn’t touch them. They were too dangerous, too politically charged, too unpopular. But for Conti, that was exactly why they needed a lawyer. “I decided to take them fully knowing that no one else would,” she said.

## Justice Isn’t Just About Winning

One of Conti’s most important messages to new lawyers is this: “Remember, not everything legal is just.” Just because you can win a case through technicalities doesn’t mean you should, and it definitely doesn’t mean justice was served.

She warns young lawyers about the seductive dangers of power and winning at all costs. Lawyering should be about fairness and the protection of rights, not just cleverness or technical wins in court. Lawyers represent clients but also serve as officers of the court—they must ensure proceedings are fair, that evidence is legitimate, and that the rule of law actually works.

When Conti represented families of drug war victims before the International Criminal Court (ICC), she wasn’t just seeking punishment for Duterte. She was trying to push back against the culture of impunity that let the powerful commit crimes without consequence. She was fighting for the principle that justice isn’t just for the winners.

## The Real Threats to Justice

Conti knows firsthand what happens when lawyers do their jobs properly in politically sensitive cases. Since the ICC issued an arrest warrant for ex-president Duterte in March 2025, she has faced an escalating campaign of red-tagging (being labeled a communist or terrorist), harassment, and threats against her and her family.

Troll armies have called her slurs, posted her photos with threatening messages, and tried to discredit her work. International organizations—including the Law Society of England and Wales, Lawyers for Lawyers, and the International Bar Association—have condemned these attacks and called on Philippine authorities to protect her.

Why does this matter for understanding the role of lawyers? Because when lawyers are threatened for representing unpopular clients or challenging power, the entire justice system breaks down. If lawyers can’t do their work without fear, then ordinary people lose their only protection against abuse.

## What Lawyers Really Owe Society

The Philippine Code of Professional Responsibility and Accountability says lawyers must uphold the Constitution and laws, promote respect for legal processes, and safeguard human rights. Lawyers in government service have an even higher duty, because public office is a public trust.

But Conti’s vision goes further. She believes lawyers must educate the public that “lawyers really work for the good of all.” It’s a noble profession—corporate lawyers, public interest lawyers, and government lawyers should all be bound by the same principles of integrity.

The problem is, legal services remain out of reach for most Filipinos. Only 20 percent can access legal aid, and many get advice from friends or family, not lawyers. The poor face barriers of cost, distance, and simply not knowing any lawyers. This is why Conti emphasizes: Justice must reach the margins, where it’s most needed.

Side Note: What I find both interesting and intriguing is the silence of the Integrated Bar of the Philippines regarding the harassment Atty. Kristina Conti is experiencing. For an organization that represents the country’s legal profession, the lack of a public statement or defense in this situation stands out. Instead, it was international legal and human rights groups that issued calls for protection and voiced concern over the threats against her.

## Defending Rights, Not Crimes

When you see a lawyer defending someone accused of terrible things, understand what’s actually happening. That lawyer isn’t saying the crime was okay. They’re defending the Constitution, due process, and your rights—even if you never need them.

As Conti puts it, she’s “a human rights activist who just happens to be a lawyer.” Her tool is the law, but her commitment is to people—especially those on the margins.

In developing countries like the Philippines, lawyers are often seen as corrupt enablers of an unfair system. But when legal systems are broken, it weakens the rule of law for everyone. That’s why public education is so critical, and why lawyers like Conti keep pushing for it.

## What Can You Do?

- Share accurate, fact-checked information on what lawyers actually do
- Listen to stories from public interest lawyers and their clients
- Support or donate to legal aid organizations
- Push for policy reform and better legal services for the marginalized

Justice needs more than punishing the accused. It needs lawyers who fight for fairness, constitutional rights, and equal treatment for all. Atty. Kristina Conti stands in this tradition—her work helps make these ideals more than just words on a page, but a reality in people’s life.

Sometimes you stumble upon a story that makes you stop and think: how can something as pure as love become such a legal mess?

A woman walks away from a husband who hurt her, builds a new life, and finds love again—the kind that doesn't break. She and her new partner have three kids who carry his eyes, his patience, and his last name on everything except paper.

When she goes to register their children, the clerk tells her something that feels almost absurd: they can't use their father's name. They have to use the name of her ex-husband. Because technically, she's still married to him.

That's how it works here. Even if a man disappears, even if a woman escapes violence, the law insists the first marriage still exists. It's called the “presumption of legitimacy”—a rule that assumes any child born to a married woman is automatically her husband's. ([Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/bar/2025/civil-law/family-code/marriage/paternity-and-filiation/legitimate-children), [Lawyer Philippines](https://www.lawyer-philippines.com/articles/legitimacy-of-a-child-born-during-the-waiting-period-between-court-decision-and-certificate-of-finality)). No questions asked, no DNA tests, no exceptions.

And that's where love starts losing to the system.

## Stuck in Paper Chains: Naming Children in a Broken Marriage

This story was shared with us by a family friend, and I'm writing about it because her story moved me.

At City Hall, she fills out the forms carefully, ready to give her kids the name of the man who’s been there for every first step and every fever. The clerk says it flatly: “Sorry, ma’am, you’re still married. The children have to use your husband’s name.”

It doesn’t matter that the husband in question hasn’t been around for years. It doesn’t matter that there’s a different father who provides for them every single day. What matters, at least to the system, is what’s printed on a piece of paper.

Under Philippine law, any child born to a married woman is automatically presumed to be her husband’s. It’s called the “presumption of legitimacy,” and it’s written in the Family Code. It doesn’t ask for DNA tests or reality; it only asks for the legal status on file ([Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/bar/2025/civil-law/family-code/marriage/paternity-and-filiation/legitimate-children), [Lawyer Philippines](https://www.lawyer-philippines.com/articles/legitimacy-of-a-child-born-during-the-waiting-period-between-court-decision-and-certificate-of-finality)).

The intention was once good: to protect children from being labeled “illegitimate.” But somewhere along the way, it became a trap. Kids grow up carrying a name that doesn’t belong to their story. Their real father disappears from their birth certificates, replaced by someone who walked away long ago. And the mother, after surviving abuse or abandonment, ends up tied to her past through paperwork she never asked for ([Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/dear-attorney/determining-the-surname-of-a-child-born-out-of-a-relationship-while-married-but-separated), [Philippine Law Firm](https://philippinelawfirm.com/family/legitimate-and-illegitimate-children/)).

It’s a quiet kind of cruelty—the kind that hides behind rules written decades ago and calls it protection.

## Annulment: Pricey, Painful, and Pathetically Slow

When people hear about stories like this, someone always says, “Then why not just get an annulment?”

If only it were that simple.

In this country, freedom from a broken marriage doesn’t come cheap. An annulment in the Philippines can cost anywhere from ₱150,000 to ₱725,000 — sometimes more, depending on how complicated the case is ([Montano Flamiano Law](https://montanoflamianolaw.com/annulment-cost-in-the-philippines/), [Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/commentaries/annulment-process-in-the-philippines-costs-and-requirements), [Annulment.ph](http://annulment.ph/), [DeBorja Law](https://deborjalaw.com/cost-of-annulment-in-the-philippines/)).

Lawyers take the biggest chunk, usually up to ₱600,000 for more difficult cases, and many require upfront retainers before a single paper even reaches the courtroom. Then come the psychological evaluations — another ₱25,000 to ₱100,000. You have to pay a licensed psychologist to testify that your marriage was doomed from the start, usually under the ground of “psychological incapacity” ([Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/features/annulling-marriages-based-on-psychological-incapacity), [Montano Flamiano Law](https://montanoflamianolaw.com/long-absence-as-psychological-incapacity-in-marriage/), [Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/features/psychological-incapacity-in-the-philippines)).

Add the smaller expenses — filing fees, publication costs when the ex can’t be found, and the endless transportation to and from hearings that can stretch on for years. It’s exhausting and expensive, and for most single parents, it’s simply impossible.

And after all that, there’s still no certainty that the court will grant it. The burden of proof is incredibly high, requiring witnesses, documentation, and a mountain of patience. Even with all that, some judges still reject petitions for not meeting the “strict legal grounds” ([Legal Resource PH](https://legalresource.ph/psychological-incapacity-a36-family-code/), [Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/features/psychological-incapacity-in-the-philippines)).

While waiting, you’re still legally married. You can’t remarry, you can’t give your children their true father’s name, and you can’t fully move forward — not completely. Life continues, but on paper, you’re frozen in the same chapter you tried so hard to leave.

## The Last Holdout: Why We Can’t Just Get a Divorce

Here’s where the heartbreak turns into policy failure. The Philippines — aside from the Vatican — remains the only place in the entire world where divorce isn’t allowed for ordinary citizens. Everywhere else, people can file, rebuild, and start over without having to wait.

For years, lawmakers have gone back and forth on the divorce bill, each time stopping short of passing it into law. The House of Representatives finally approved it in 2024, but it’s been sitting in the Senate ever since, stuck between debates about “family values” and “moral decay” ([PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1225339), [ABC News](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-09-21/why-filipinos-are-campaigning-to-legalise-divorce/104331936), [PCW](https://pcw.gov.ph/assets/files/2020/03/PCW-WPLA-PB-09-Adopting-Divorce-in-the-Family-Code.pdf)).

The Catholic Church has always been firm in opposing it, warning that divorce would destroy families. But many argue that it is the absence of divorce that breaks them apart in silence. Women remain trapped in abusive marriages, children grow up in legal limbo, and couples who simply grew apart are forced to stay bound on paper even when the relationship has been dead for years ([Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2024/9/30/in-the-philippines-costly-marriage-annulments-spur-calls-to-allow-divorce), [IGG Geo](https://igg-geo.org/en/2023/06/27/divorce-prohibition-in-the-philippines-a-system-serving-the-patriarchy/), [UP CIDS](https://cids.up.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/The-Divorce-Debate-in-the-Philippines_Actors-Stakeholders-Issues-Challenges-and-Capacities.pdf)).

Other Catholic nations — Italy, Spain, even Ireland — eventually recognized that protecting marriage isn’t the same as trapping the people in it. Yet here, the same argument is repeated over and over by leaders who’ve likely never had to live through that kind of trap.

So while the rest of the world moves forward, we stay the last holdout, clinging to an illusion of sanctity while thousands of families quietly live in pain.

## Who Suffers? Always the Women—and Their Kids

It’s almost always the mothers who pay the highest price. Women who survive abuse, neglect, or years of separation still carry the burden long after they’ve walked away. They raise their children alone, often without financial support, while the law makes it harder for them to move on.

Family courts move slowly. Child support cases drag for years. And because the country has no divorce law, many women have no clean way to rebuild their lives. They can’t remarry, can’t change their children’s surnames, and can’t legally protect their new families from the legal shadow of the old one ([PCW](https://pcw.gov.ph/assets/files/2020/03/PCW-WPLA-PB-09-Adopting-Divorce-in-the-Family-Code.pdf), [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2024/9/30/in-the-philippines-costly-marriage-annulments-spur-calls-to-allow-divorce)).

And the children? They grow up with papers that tell a story that isn’t theirs. Every time they apply for a passport, fill out a school form, or request a birth certificate, they’re reminded that the law calls someone else their father. The man who was actually there — present for every scraped knee, every award ceremony, every night of fever — he doesn’t exist in the eyes of the law ([NDV Law](https://ndvlaw.com/if-the-wife-gives-birth-to-a-child-of-another-man-what-is-the-status-of-the-child/), [Respicio & Co.](https://www.respicio.ph/commentaries/legal-rights-of-children-born-out-of-wedlock-in-the-philippines)).

This is the part no one in Congress talks about. For them, it’s all about “protecting the sanctity of marriage.” But for the women and children trapped in this system, it’s about survival — about trying to live with dignity in a country that values a piece of paper more than a real family’s peace.

## Why the Law Needs to Change, Now

A family friend once told me her story, and it hasn’t left my mind since. She’s one of many trying to build a good life while the laws keep them tied to the past. Every time a mother signs a birth certificate with the wrong surname, or every time a woman can’t move on because the papers say she’s still married, the country turns its back on her.

The divorce bill finally passed the House in 2024 — a step forward that went nowhere fast. It’s been stuck in the Senate, buried under the usual noise of politics, moral posturing, and delays ([PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1225339), [ABC News](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-09-21/why-filipinos-are-campaigning-to-legalise-divorce/104331936)). Meanwhile, the people waiting for change are already running out of years to wait.

Real reform doesn’t need slogans; it needs compassion.

• Pass a divorce law that’s humane and affordable.
• Shorten and simplify annulments so families can start over.
• Allow birth records to be corrected without involving an estranged spouse.
• Build real support systems for survivors — places that help, not hotlines that barely work.

These laws were written for a time when obedience was mistaken for virtue. They assume marriages fail only because people give up too soon, when in truth, most have already endured far too much. Divorce doesn’t destroy families; abuse, abandonment, indifference, and fear do ([IGG Geo](https://igg-geo.org/en/2023/06/27/divorce-prohibition-in-the-philippines-a-system-serving-the-patriarchy/), [UP CIDS](https://cids.up.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/The-Divorce-Debate-in-the-Philippines_Actors-Stakeholders-Issues-Challenges-and-Capacities.pdf)).

We’ve confused endurance with strength for generations. Real strength is knowing when it’s time to choose peace instead of pain. Families built on love shouldn’t have to fight the law to be recognized.

## A Quiet Reflection

I think about her sometimes — how she carries herself with calm despite everything. No bitterness, just a quiet acceptance of how unfair the system can be. It’s strange how, in this country, you can follow all the rules and still be the one who loses.

Maybe that’s what love looks like here. It keeps going even when the law doesn’t make room for it. It exists in the small things — in laughter at the dinner table, in school projects pinned to the wall, in the kind of home built with effort, not just luck.

We like to say that family is sacred. If that’s true, then the law should treat it that way — not as paperwork, not as a moral debate, but as something real, fragile, and human.

Because at the end of it all, people don’t ask for much. Just the freedom to choose peace, and the right to live with the truth written under their own name.

I remember reading about the Cambridge Analytica issue back in 2016 but never really got around to understanding how it actually changed things in the Philippines. The scandal showed how technology could quietly reshape politics, influence perception, and tilt the scales without most people even knowing it was happening. As 2028 draws closer and Vice President Sara Duterte’s name surfaces in talks about the next presidential race, the lessons from that era feel more relevant than ever.

## What Cambridge Analytica Claimed — and What Officials Denied

Strategic Communications Laboratories (SCL), the parent company of Cambridge Analytica, once took credit for a presidential win in the Philippines. An archived version of their website, as reported by the [South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140303/how-cambridge-analyticas-parent-company-helped-man-action), described how the firm supposedly “rebranded” an unnamed candidate after discovering that Filipino voters were looking for a strong, decisive leader. The site claimed SCL used crime as a “cross-cutting issue” to frame its client as a no-nonsense leader who could restore order ([Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/cambridge-analytica-duterte-strongman-2016/), [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/cambridge-analytica-duterte-tough-guy-image-for-presidential-election-2018-4)).

But every official linked to Duterte’s camp denied any connection. Presidential spokesperson Harry Roque and other campaign officials maintained that they never hired the firm ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/world/philippines-leader-did-not-buy-data-to-win-election-spokesman-idUSKBN1HH1B0/), [PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2018/04/14/1805765/duterte-denies-tapping-cambridge-analytica), [PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1031976)). To this day, no contract, invoice, or payment record has surfaced proving that Duterte’s campaign ever paid for Cambridge Analytica’s services.

## The Connections That Started It All

While no formal contract has ever been proven, the trail of connections between Cambridge Analytica and the 2016 campaign is hard to ignore. In May 2015, a year before the election, Cambridge Analytica CEO Alexander Nix flew to Manila and gave a presentation on data-driven campaigning. Photos later emerged showing him dining with key figures from the Duterte camp, including Peter Tiu Laviña and Pompee La Viña ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140782/cambridge-analytica-boss-alexander-nix-dined-two-rodrigo)).

La Viña, who would become Duterte’s social media director, confirmed the meeting but said they merely discussed digital trends and never signed a deal ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2142805/explainer-connections-between-philippine-president-rodrigo)). An SCL brochure from 2013 also listed Istratehiya—a political consulting firm partly owned by Millard Lim, another Duterte ally—as its local partner ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2142805/explainer-connections-between-philippine-president-rodrigo)). The missing link is still the same: there’s no record of payment or contract connecting Cambridge Analytica directly to the candidate himself.

## The Digital Footprint That Followed

What’s undisputed is the scale of data collection that took place. Cambridge Analytica harvested information from over 1.17 million Facebook users in the Philippines—the second-largest breach globally after the United States ([Straits Times](https://www.straitstimes.com/asia/se-asia/harvested-facebook-data-used-to-elect-duterte), [Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/technology/philippines-watchdog-probes-facebook-over-cambridge-analytica-data-breach-idUSKBN1HK0T8/), [Privacy Commission](https://privacy.gov.ph/press-statement-from-privacy-commissioner-raymund-enriquez-liboro-on-the-facebook-controversy-involving-cambridge-analytica/)). The Philippine National Privacy Commission launched an investigation after Facebook confirmed the breach ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/982151/npc-national-privacy-commission-data-breach-facebook-cambridge-analytica-philippines)). Yet the probe never established how the data was actually used—or if it was used for local politics at all.

Another confirmed element was the $200,000 spent on a troll army during Duterte’s campaign. A University of Oxford study found that the campaign employed between 400 and 500 people to spread propaganda and attack opponents online ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2017/07/24/1721044/duterte-camp-spent-200000-troll-army-oxford-study-finds)). Duterte later acknowledged it himself, saying, “When I was a candidate, I spent for trolls… They were only for the elections” ([Global Voices](https://advox.globalvoices.org/2017/08/08/troll-in-chief-philippine-president-rodrigo-duterte-admits-hiring-online-defenders-during-2016-election/), [BusinessWorld](https://www.bworldonline.com/the-nation/2017/07/26/19491/president-confirms-trolls-2016/)). What remains today is less about proof of payment and more about influence—the quiet kind that doesn’t need a signature to leave a mark.

## The Confirmed Disinformation Infrastructure

The troll networks that emerged in 2016 didn’t vanish when the campaign ended. They grew into something more sophisticated. A report from the NATO Strategic Communications Centre of Excellence (StratComCOE) detailed how these networks became institutionalized ([StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)).

The study identified four main models of disinformation production. The first involves state-sponsored operations, where government communication teams directly control propaganda work. Many creators of pro-Duterte content during the campaign eventually found positions in government, particularly within the Presidential Communications Operations Office (PCOO) ([StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)).

The second type includes in-house campaign staff—employees who manage fake accounts as part of their regular duties. Next are advertising and PR consultants brought in as short-term specialists. These professionals offer digital strategy services that blur the lines between legitimate marketing and covert manipulation. The fourth type consists of clickbait operations that generate political content purely for ad revenue. These operators focus less on ideology and more on engagement, often spreading sensational or fake news to drive traffic.

These models often overlap, forming a full-spectrum propaganda network that spans official pages, influencer accounts, and anonymous troll armies. The machinery born in 2016 was no longer just a campaign weapon—it had become a business model.

## The Broader Context: Marcos and Historical Revisionism

The Cambridge Analytica story doesn’t end with Duterte. In 2020, whistleblower Brittany Kaiser, a former director at the firm, claimed that Bongbong Marcos had also reached out to the company to help “rebrand” the Marcos family image. Kaiser explained that the work would be done in a “data-driven and scientific way,” through research and psychographic profiling ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brittany_Kaiser), [Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/bongbong-marcos-disinformation-social-media-philippines-president/)).

Marcos’s spokesperson immediately denied the claim, calling it “patently fake, false, and misleading” ([ABS-CBN](https://www.abs-cbn.com/news/07/16/20/bongbong-requested-cambridge-analytica-to-rebrand-family-image-says-whistleblower-marcos-denies-claim)). No payment records or contracts have surfaced, and Cambridge Analytica’s own archives don’t confirm a Marcos project. Still, the results speak for themselves. Years before his 2022 presidential victory, a massive online campaign was already underway to reshape public memory of the Martial Law era, portraying it as a “golden age” ([Vice](https://www.vice.com/en/article/bongbong-marcos-disinformation-social-media-philippines-president/), [IRGAC](https://irgac.org/articles/the-2022-philippine-president-elections-are-over-when-will-the-fake-news-stop)).

By 2022, the disinformation strategy had matured into a coordinated digital operation. Research found that Marcos benefited most from these narratives, which were spread through a vast network of influencers and social media accounts ([UP OVPPAA Study](https://ovpaa.up.edu.ph/research/social-media-fueled-widespread-dissemination-of-disinformation-narratives-during-the-2022-philippine-national-election-campaign-period/)). What began as a digital effort to manage public perception eventually turned into a larger project of historical revisionism.

## The Current Landscape: AI, Deepfakes, and Evolved Tactics

Cambridge Analytica officially shut down in 2018 after the global data scandal, but the playbook it created has only become more advanced ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambridge_Analytica), [NPR](https://www.npr.org/sections/thetwo-way/2018/05/02/607782799/cambridge-analytica-is-shutting-down-after-facebook-data-controversy), [GMA News](https://www.gmanetwork.com/news/money/companies/652058/cambridge-analytica-and-parent-scl-elections-shutting-down/story/)). In the Philippines, the techniques once used have evolved. Disinformation now relies less on simple text posts and more on sophisticated multimedia content.

A report from the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism (PCIJ) identified recurring tactics of the "Duterte influence machine," including the use of emotional manipulation and AI-generated content ([PCIJ, August 07, 2025](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)). The use of AI has become especially troubling. In June 2025, Senator Bato dela Rosa admitted to using an AI avatar of himself to communicate with voters. When questioned, Vice President Sara Duterte defended its use, saying there was “no problem” with using AI to generate support for her ([PhilStar, June 16, 2025](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2025/06/16/2451010/sara-duterte-sees-no-problem-using-ai-generate-support-her)).

The normalization of deepfakes in political discourse is also alarming. A recent study by Cyabra found a surge in disinformation favoring the Dutertes during political tensions ([Reporting ASEAN](https://www.reportingasean.net/philippines-duterte-arrest-sparks-disinformation-surge-in-his-favour/)). Other analyses show how these operations extend beyond domestic politics. The Manila Times reported on secret troll farms allegedly linked to Chinese influence operations ([Manila Times, October 08, 2025](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)). In response, President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. ordered a formal investigation into suspected Chinese influence ([Manila Times](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)).

## Sara Duterte and the 2028 Presidential Race

Vice President Sara Duterte continues to lead early surveys for the 2028 presidential election ([Time](https://time.com/6588859/sara-duterte-philippines-president-2028-polls/), [PhilStar Life](https://philstarlife.com/living/465068-sara-duterte-bong-top-choice-president-vice-president-2028)). She has also received her father’s blessing, with Rodrigo Duterte telling her to "run if you want to" during a visit to The Hague ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2085664/sara-duterte-gets-dads-nod-for-possible-2028-election-run-run-if-you-want-to)).

Despite her high ranking in polls, Sara’s road to 2028 has not been smooth. In February 2025, 240 mid-level influencers who previously supported the administration reportedly shifted their allegiance toward the Marcos camp ([PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)). Meanwhile, Partido Demokratiko Pilipino (PDP) has been courting her to be its standard-bearer ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2111240/sara-duterte-says-pdp-persistent-in-courting-her-for-2028-elections)). The stage is being set early, with loyal networks online and old allies positioning for the race.

## Will Sara Duterte Use Similar Tactics in 2028?

Several signs suggest that Sara Duterte’s 2028 campaign could rely on digital manipulation strategies. The infrastructure that once fueled her father's campaigns is still intact, with many of the same influencers and PR professionals active ([PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/), [StratComCOE Report](https://stratcomcoe.org/cuploads/pfiles/5december_report_philippines.pdf)). Her public stance on AI-generated political content also raises concern ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2025/06/16/2451010/sara-duterte-sees-no-problem-using-ai-generate-support-her)).

Regulation remains weak. While the Commission on Elections (Comelec) released guidelines for AI and social media in September 2024 (Resolution No. 11064), critics argue these measures are insufficient to stop large-scale disinformation ([NDV Law](https://ndvlaw.com/regulation-of-digital-campaign-in-the-2025-philippine-elections/), [Baker McKenzie InsightPlus](https://insightplus.bakermckenzie.com/bm/technology-media-telecommunications_1/philippines-ai-and-social-media-guidelines-for-the-2025-elections-issued-by-the-comelec)). The political stakes are also enormous, with Rodrigo Duterte facing charges before the ICC, making a 2028 win a matter of political survival ([Inquirer](https://newsinfo.inquirer.net/2085664/sara-duterte-gets-dads-nod-for-possible-2028-election-run-run-if-you-want-to), [PCIJ](https://pcij.org/2025/08/07/five-ways-the-duterte-influence-machine-is-deceiving-filipinos/)).

The Philippines remains fertile ground for disinformation, with around 87 million active social media users and high levels of screen time ([New Internationalist](https://newint.org/science-and-technology/2024/philippines-disinformation-machine)). Public awareness is higher now, and fact-checking organizations are more coordinated ([PCIJ, June 13, 2025](https://pcij.org/2025/06/13/2025-philippine-elections-blog-midterm-polls/), [PNA](https://www.pna.gov.ph/articles/1243295)), but their reach still pales compared to viral propaganda networks. The ongoing rivalry between the Marcos and Duterte camps further complicates the picture as competing narratives flood social media ([Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/7/15/ai-and-disinformation-prompt-political-rivalries-in-the-philippines), [Manila Times](https://www.manilatimes.net/2025/10/08/news/national/inside-the-troll-farm-chinas-secret-campaign-to-sway-philippine-opinion-and-undermine-marcos/2196262)).

## Conclusion: Separating Facts from Claims in the Disinformation Age

The Cambridge Analytica story in the Philippines shows how easily the lines between fact, influence, and manipulation can blur. Here’s what we know for sure:
- Over 1.17 million Filipinos had their Facebook data harvested by Cambridge Analytica ([Privacy Commission](https://privacy.gov.ph/press-statement-from-privacy-commissioner-raymund-enriquez-liboro-on-the-facebook-controversy-involving-cambridge-analytica/)).
- Around $200,000 was spent on a 400 to 500-person troll army during Duterte’s 2016 campaign ([PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2017/07/24/1721044/duterte-camp-spent-200000-troll-army-oxford-study-finds)).
- Cambridge Analytica’s CEO met with future Duterte campaign figures in 2015, and SCL’s archived website claimed credit for rebranding him into a “strongman” candidate ([South China Morning Post](https://www.scmp.com/news/asia/southeast-asia/article/2140782/cambridge-analytica-boss-alexander-nix-dined-two-rodrigo), [Business Insider](https://www.businessinsider.com/cambridge-analytica-duterte-tough-guy-image-for-presidential-election-2018-4)).
- All Philippine officials involved have denied any business dealings with the firm ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/world/philippines-leader-did-not-buy-data-to-win-election-spokesman-idUSKBN1HH1B0/), [PhilStar](https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2018/04/14/1805765/duterte-denies-tapping-cambridge-analytica)).

What remains uncertain is whether Cambridge Analytica was ever formally hired or paid by Duterte’s campaign. Its boasts could have been based on real consulting work, exaggerated self-promotion, or indirect collaboration through intermediaries. What matters more is how the system it inspired has endured. The psychographic profiling, emotional targeting, and anonymous online armies that were revolutionary in 2016 have become standard practice. Whether or not a Cambridge Analytica successor exists today, its influence does. The question is whether voters will see the patterns before they are repeated in 2028.
