The idea came one quiet morning while I was getting ready for the day. A question appeared out of nowhere: why does it feel like critical thinking is dying in the Philippines? Then another one followed—was it ever really alive here?

I paused, thinking about how we confuse information with understanding. Everyone has something to say, but how many are actually thinking? We scroll, we react, we argue. But rarely do we stop to ask, wait, totoo ba ‘to?

It stayed with me for the rest of the day. I kept replaying that question while doing the laundry, while eating lunch, and even while staring at a blank screen. I realized that "critical thinking" is a phrase we throw around a lot in schools and on social media, but we rarely define what it means—or why it’s so hard to practice here.

## What Is Critical Thinking? A Philosophical Foundation

Before asking whether critical thinking ever lived or died in our country, I had to stop and ask what it actually is. It’s not just being "smart" or winning an argument.

Critical thinking is the process of analyzing facts, evidence, observations, and arguments to form a judgment. It’s the ability to think clearly and rationally, understanding the logical connection between ideas.

American philosopher John Dewey gave this habit a modern name in his 1910 book How We Think. He called it “reflective thinking”—a disciplined way of pausing before judgment, keeping a healthy skepticism, and following evidence where it leads.

But the roots go much further back—long before psychology departments or research journals. The story begins in Athens.

## The Ancient Roots: Socrates, Plato, and the Birth of Dialectic

The story of critical thinking begins with Socrates (470–399 BCE), the Athenian philosopher who spent his life asking questions no one else wanted to answer. He didn’t claim to have the truth; he claimed only to know that he knew nothing.

From this, Socrates drew one lasting insight: authority is not the same as truth. A person can lead a city or a church and still be wrong.

What became known as the Socratic Method—or elenchus—was a disciplined way of asking questions. Socrates would challenge assumptions, test definitions, and expose contradictions. He wanted his students to reach a state of aporia, that uneasy state of realizing one’s ignorance. That moment wasn’t meant to embarrass anyone; it was the starting line for real learning.

In today’s language, it’s like a respectful debate where both sides keep asking “why?” until the surface answers fall apart. It’s the opposite of the kind of arguing that happens on social media, where people start with their conclusions and work backward to find insults.

In ancient Athens, this habit set Socrates apart from the Sophists, the celebrity teachers of his time who taught the art of persuasion for money. They trained people to win arguments, not to find truth. They used eristic and antilogical methods, techniques that sounded smart but often confused more than they clarified. Socrates refused to be one of them.

His student Plato (428–347 BCE) recorded those conversations and expanded what Socrates began. He developed the dialectic method—a cooperative form of dialogue where people test ideas through reasoned discussion rather than emotional reaction.

(SIDE NOTE: It’s the same kind of exchange I try to create in the comment section of Morning Coffee Thoughts. Trying being the operative word, because keeping a space respectful and curious online is harder than it looks.)

Plato believed reason could harmonize opposing views, and that a just society required citizens capable of this level of thought.

Then came Aristotle (384–322 BCE), Plato’s student, who transformed logic into a formal system. He created the syllogism—a form of reasoning where a conclusion is drawn from two given or assumed premises. For example:

All humans are mortal. Socrates is human. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.

It sounds simple now, but at the time, it was groundbreaking. Aristotle mapped out how reasoning works, identifying fallacies—the "short circuits" in logic that make an argument look strong when it’s actually weak.

Socrates questioned, Plato recorded, and Aristotle organized. Together, they gave the world a way to use reason as a tool for survival.

## The Modern Evolution: From Bacon to Kant to Dewey

The Renaissance cracked open a new way of thinking: test the world, don’t just quote it. From there, critical thinking became more than just logic—it became a way of exploring reality.

Francis Bacon (1561–1626) pushed back against the old habit of leaning on pure deduction. In Novum Organum (The New Instrument, 1620), he proposed inductive reasoning—the process of drawing general principles from many small observations—rather than starting with fixed dogmas and forcing the world to fit them.

If you’ve been reading Morning Coffee Thoughts for a while, you’ve probably noticed that I tend to write this way. I start from something ordinary—a news headline, a conversation, or even a cup of coffee—and try to see where it leads. That’s inductive reasoning in action. You begin with what’s real and concrete, then slowly build toward understanding. It’s not about having all the answers at the start; it’s about having the patience to look closely.

Bacon named the “Four Idols” that distort our view: the Idols of the Tribe (human bias), the Cave (personal prejudice), the Marketplace (confusions of language), and the Theatre (dogmatic systems treated as absolute truth). These idols still stalk our feeds and classrooms today.

René Descartes (1596–1650) answered confusion with methodic doubt—a systematic way of questioning everything that could possibly be doubted until only certainty remains. In his Discourse on the Method and Meditations on First Philosophy, he stripped away all unreliable knowledge—tradition, sensory perception, even mathematics—until only one truth remained: cogito, ergo sum (“I think, therefore I am”).

He doubted authority because authorities disagree. He doubted perception because dreams and illusions can feel real. By doubting everything, he found a foundation for reasoning that no one could take away.

Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) mapped the limits of reason. In the Critique of Pure Reason (1781), he explored metaphysics—questions about what exists beyond the physical world—and asked how far the human mind can truly go. He argued that while reason is our best tool, it’s not infinite. We see the world through the "lens" of our own minds, which means we must always be aware of our own limitations.

Kant didn’t reject reason; he trained it to recognize its limits. That humility is part of critical thinking too.

The Enlightenment (often called The Age of Reason) gathered all these threads. Thinkers championed freedom, inquiry, and skepticism toward authority. They believed that if people were free to think for themselves, they could build a better world.

John Dewey (1859–1952) carried this spirit into modern education. He described critical thinking as reflective thinking—pausing before judgment, examining the process, and testing conclusions in community. For Dewey, thinking wasn’t just a private act; it was a democratic responsibility.

For him, problem-solving alone wasn’t enough. He wanted students to examine how they solved problems, not just that they did. Reflection turns trial and error into learning, and discussion keeps that reflection honest.

## Critical Thinking in Philippine History: Was It Ever There?

This is where the question turns inward: was critical thinking ever truly part of our history, or did we just import the name?

The answer isn’t simple. Long before colonizers arrived, the Philippines already had structured societies with their own logic. We had the alibata (or baybayin) as a writing system, a network of barangays that practiced collective decision-making, and active trade with our neighbors. Our ancestors were thinkers, navigators, and diplomats.

The Spanish colonial period (1565–1898) reshaped how Filipinos thought—and not for the better. Spanish friars controlled education, teaching mostly catechism, obedience, and the Spanish language. The goal was not to nurture thought, but to preserve submission. Native knowledge systems were erased or branded as pagan. Historical records written by missionaries portrayed Filipinos as “uncivilized” souls waiting for salvation. Inquiry was discouraged because curiosity could lead to rebellion.

When the Americans arrived in 1898, they brought a different kind of schooling—more accessible, but still colonial at its core. The new public education system made English the medium of instruction and built universities that mirrored American ones. It looked progressive, but as historian Renato Constantino argued in his essay The Miseducation of the Filipino, it was a calculated way to create “good colonials”: Filipinos who thought like Americans and saw their colonizers as saviors.

Constantino wrote that English became “the wedge that separated the Filipinos from their past and later to separate educated Filipinos from the masses.” Our textbooks celebrated Washington, Lincoln, and Jefferson, but called Filipino revolutionaries “bandits.” Songs, stories, and even the idea of beauty became foreign. The water cure torture and the reconcentration camps under American occupation barely appeared in class materials.

This mindset hardened into colonial mentality—the belief that anything foreign, especially Western, is automatically better. That legacy still shows up today: schools named after Spanish saints and American presidents, English as the “respectable” language, and Western literature dominating reading lists. Studies describe this as an internalized inferiority, quietly passed from one generation to the next.

Our classrooms then—and often still today—reward memorization and obedience more than analysis. A Reddit post once described it bluntly: the system “breeds obedient workers, not critical thinkers.” Students learn early that questioning a teacher risks being labeled pasaway. So they study to pass, not to understand. They memorize answers, not methods. They learn silence early and carry it into adulthood.

Colonial education left us fluent in English but hesitant in thought. It taught us to imitate before we could innovate. We learned how to speak well, but not how to question well. And that gap—between eloquence and depth—still shapes our politics, our classrooms, and our daily arguments online.

## The Current State: Measuring the Crisis

If you want to understand where we stand today, the numbers tell the story—and it isn’t pretty.

The Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) 2022 ranked the Philippines near the bottom in reading, math, and science. PISA is an international exam conducted by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)—a global body of 38 member countries that sets education benchmarks used to compare learning outcomes worldwide.

Only 24% of Filipino students reached Level 2 or higher in reading literacy, the minimum proficiency for understanding long texts or distinguishing fact from opinion. The global average is 74%. The Philippines scored 353 in reading, far below the OECD average of 476 and Singapore’s 543.

In creative thinking, Filipino students ranked among the weakest in the world, with an average score of 14 compared to the OECD average of 33. This test measures the ability to generate original ideas and evaluate them—the very heart of critical thinking.

The gap isn’t small. Only around 3% of Filipino students performed at the same level as the average student in top-performing countries. Results in mathematics (355) and science (356) show the same pattern: far below the OECD averages of 472 and 485 respectively.

Beyond numbers, research reveals what those results mean. A study of 300 junior high school students showed that while they were good at recognizing different viewpoints, they struggled with planning and organizing information—key parts of critical thinking. In short, they could accept differences but couldn’t process or structure them into a logical argument.

Another study found that although critical thinking appears in the curriculum, it isn’t effectively taught. Classrooms rely heavily on lectures and repetition. Teachers face too many students, too few resources, and a system that prioritizes "finishing the module" over deep learning. Many lack training in reflective pedagogy (the art and method of teaching), leaving critical thinking as a concept on paper rather than a practice in the classroom.

The government often talks about improving “competitiveness” or “21st-century skills,” but the real crisis is deeper than skills. It’s about our ability to discern what is true.

When we see fake news shared without pause or policies supported without scrutiny, these numbers matter. They aren’t just scores on a test; they are a warning about our future.

## Why Critical Thinking Remains Rare: Cultural and Structural Barriers

We’ve seen how our history built an education system that values obedience. The harder question is why it’s so hard to change it today.

### Inherited Habits of Obedience

The same forces that shaped our classrooms a century ago still echo today. From Spanish catechism to American textbooks, our institutions were built to produce "good subjects," not critical citizens.

We still see it in classrooms where questioning is mistaken for disrespect, in offices where compliance is rewarded over innovation, and in political circles where loyalty is more important than logic.

Pakikisama (getting along) and respeto (respect) keep relationships peaceful but often at the cost of honest dialogue. Hiya (shame) discourages public disagreement, while amor propio (self-respect or dignity) can sometimes make people too guarded to accept criticism.

These cultural values aren’t the problem. The problem is how they’re used to discourage analysis. To think critically, you must be willing to be "wrong," to be "disrespectful" to a false authority, and to break the silence of the group. In our culture, those are often seen as social sins.

Our collective mindset still leans outward too. Colonial mentality—the belief that foreign systems are always better—discourages us from developing our own intellectual traditions. We wait for foreign experts to tell us what to think instead of trusting our own capacity to analyze our own problems.

### Critical Pedagogy

Critical pedagogy means teaching students not just what to know but how to question what they know. It’s a method that encourages reflection on power, context, and meaning. It’s based on the work of thinkers like Paulo Freire, who saw education as a form of liberation rather than obedience.

In the Philippines, this approach remains rare. Teachers are expected to finish modules, not start debates. The Community of Inquiry model—where students analyze, agree, and disagree without hostility—barely exists in local classrooms. Without this practice, students never learn how to disagree without being disagreeable.

Critical pedagogy works best when teachers have the time and trust to guide open conversations. But in our system, those are luxuries many don't have.

### The Teacher’s Burden

Many Filipino teachers never learned to teach critical thinking because they were never taught to think critically themselves.

Studies show that low critical thinking skills among pre-service teachers (students training to become teachers) directly affect the quality of education that follows. Without a solid foundation in reasoning, teachers naturally fall back on what they know: lectures, memorization, and obedience.

The cycle feeds itself: teachers who weren’t trained to question now teach students not to question. Breaking this cycle requires more than just new modules; it requires a shift in how we train the people who lead our classrooms.

## The Digital Double-Edged Sword: Internet, Social Media, and Critical Thinking

The rise of the internet and social media gave Filipinos access to more information than any generation before. But it also brought a new kind of crisis.

### The Crisis: Disinformation as “Patient Zero”

The Philippines has been called “patient zero” in the global misinformation epidemic—a reference to how early and fast disinformation spread here. With our high social media usage and low reading literacy, we were the perfect environment for it.

Surveys show that 67% of Filipinos view disinformation as a serious national problem. Fake news sites have existed here since at least 2005, often used to attack journalists or political rivals.

During the 2016 presidential campaign, disinformation became industrial. Political teams paid troll networks to flood timelines with pro-Duterte content while attacking anyone who questioned him. By the 2022 elections, the machinery was fully professionalized, portraying Ferdinand “Bongbong” Marcos Jr. as the restorer of a “golden age” while downplaying the violence and theft of martial law.

Researchers call this the “architecture of networked disinformation”—a structured industry of political consultants, influencers, and “click armies” running coordinated campaigns. It’s not just random people sharing lies; it’s a business.

When Rodrigo Duterte was arrested in 2025 for alleged crimes against humanity, within hours, hundreds of pages posted identical scripts calling it a "foreign conspiracy." These posts weren’t meant to inform; they were meant to confuse and drown out the facts.

This system thrives precisely because our schools and culture haven’t trained us to pause, verify, and question. Disinformation doesn’t just spread because of technology; it spreads because of a lack of critical thinking.

### The Opportunity: Social Media as a Tool for Thinking

Yet the same platforms that spread lies can also strengthen reasoning. Research shows that when students use social media for study and discussion, their critical thinking improves. The key difference is purpose: why and how we use it.

Students who join academic discussions online, share insights, or cross-check data with peers tend to develop better analytical skills. They learn to see different angles, find sources, and defend their own ideas with evidence.

One study found a strong link between social media literacy—the ability to judge credibility and detect manipulation—and the growth of critical thinking itself. If we teach people how the platforms work, they can start using them as tools for inquiry instead of just machines for reaction.

Filipino students, despite low global scores, remain highly curious. Eight out of ten say they enjoy learning new things. The challenge is to shift how we use our feeds: less consumption, more conversation. Instead of reacting to the loudest voice, we can start following the strongest evidence.

## How Political Bloggers Can Encourage Critical Thinking

As a political blogger, I know the responsibility that comes with every post. Each sentence can either add to the noise or help clear it. Here is how I try to use Morning Coffee Thoughts to encourage real thinking:

### Lead by Example

The most powerful thing a writer can do is model discipline. I cite my sources, show my reasoning, and admit when I don’t have all the answers. If the data is incomplete, I say so. If I’m wrong, I correct it—publicly.

Readers notice consistency more than perfection. When they see you question your own assumptions, they start feeling safer to question their own. Transparency builds trust, and trust opens the door for better dialogue.

### Teach the Process, Not the Opinion

Most readers were never taught how to evaluate information. So I explain the small things: how to check a page’s source, how to use reverse image search, or how to spot a post that twists context. Sometimes I walk them through how I verified a claim so they can do the same on their own.

The goal is simple: help readers understand how to think through information instead of just reacting to it. Once people grasp the process, they start noticing weak arguments by themselves and learn to build stronger ones in return.

### Use Questions, Not Just Answers

Socrates taught by asking. His method still works online—just without the toga.

Questions like “Who benefits from this narrative?”, “What evidence would change your mind?”, or “What are we not being told?” make readers pause before reacting. These questions don’t trap anyone. They invite reflection, which is the heart of critical thinking.

### Build a Community of Inquiry

The comment section, when done right, can be a classroom. I try to keep mine that way. I highlight thoughtful comments—even when they disagree with me. I moderate—not to silence dissent, but to make space for real discussion.

There’s a difference between disagreement and distortion. I welcome readers who argue in good faith, not those who spread propaganda or repeat scripted lies. Respectful debate has a place here; coordinated disinformation does not.

Online, tone spreads faster than truth. If one person argues respectfully, others follow. That’s how a community of inquiry begins.

### Celebrate Curiosity

Curiosity is underrated. Many Filipinos—especially students—already have it. They love learning new things and asking why. It just isn’t always rewarded.

Instead of feeding certainty, I try to reward curiosity. When readers share questions, I feature them. When they find a new source, I thank them. By celebrating the act of searching, we make thinking feel like a discovery rather than a chore.

### Root It in Filipino Values

Critical thinking doesn’t cancel pakikipagkapwa-tao (regard for others) or dangal (honor). It deepens them. To think clearly is to care deeply.

I remind my readers that checking facts before sharing protects others from harm. That courage to speak truth to power is a form of dangal. And that patience to listen, even to opposing views, is a form of malasakit (compassion).

When we frame critical thinking as part of our identity—as something Filipino, not foreign—it starts to feel like common sense instead of rebellion.

### Be Patient

Critical thinking grows slowly. Most of the time, readers won’t admit right away that something changed their mind. They might just stop arguing or start asking better questions. That’s enough.

Teaching through writing means accepting that growth takes time. You don’t always see the impact, but you keep writing because you know that a single clear thought can go a long way.

## The Path Forward

After tracing the roots of critical thinking—from ancient philosophy to colonial classrooms—it’s easy to feel overwhelmed by the data. It’s a sobering picture of our present.

But I still believe we can do better. The data may be sobering, but the curiosity is still there. PISA showed that 81% of Filipino students enjoy learning new things—a number far higher than our test scores suggest. That spark is the seed of critical thinking. We just need to stop smothering it with memorization and silence.

History reminds us that critical thinking doesn’t appear naturally. Socrates built it through questioning, Bacon built it through observation, and Dewey built it through community. We can build it here, too.

Change begins where questions begin. That means rethinking how we teach, how we post, and how we talk to each other.

- Education reform should reward reasoning over memorization, so students learn to ask why before what.
- Teacher training should include the tools to guide reflection, not just lesson plans.
- Curriculum design should weave critical thinking into every subject, not treat it as a chapter in English or Social Studies.
- Digital literacy programs should help citizens recognize manipulation before it spreads.
- And cultural renewal means learning to question with respect, disagree with care, and see thinking not as defiance, but as a form of love for our country.

As bloggers, educators, or readers, we can’t fix everything overnight. But each of us can model what it looks like to pause before reacting, to verify before sharing, and to ask before assuming. Every small act of curiosity adds weight to the side of reason.

The path is long, and the noise will always be loud. But each time someone slows down to think, the silence of understanding grows a little stronger. That, to me, is where democracy begins—not in shouting, but in listening long enough to question.

The Department of Health is facing its own infrastructure mess, and it's drawing obvious parallels to DPWH's flood-control fiasco.

As of mid-October 2025, the spotlight is on hundreds of "super health centers" built with public money but still idle, abandoned, or half-finished across the country.

 300 Idle Facilities and Counting

Health Secretary Ted Herbosa told the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) that the list of non-operational super health centers has reached 300.

Out of 878 funded since 2021, only 196 are running, 17 are partially open, and the rest are either unfinished or stuck in various stages of construction.

Herbosa himself first flagged the pattern in late September during budget talks, likening the HFEP rollout to the DPWH flood-control saga. Billions were poured into local units, yet only a fraction of facilities are actually serving people.

 Marikina Becomes the Flashpoint

One case keeps coming up: the Concepcion Dos Super Health Center in Marikina got over twenty million pesos and remains an overgrown lot with footings and weeds. Herbosa's on-site remark—celebrating that DOH "exposed" the issue—triggered backlash online.

Marikina's mayor hit back, saying Phase 1 was completed and certified by DOH, but later phases stalled because the department didn't release the remaining funds.

The city has set aside its own budget to finish the job. In Antipolo, a "completed" center suddenly opened its doors ahead of Herbosa's visit, raising eyebrows about how long it had been "ready" on paper.

 ICI Steps In, Citizens' Audit Follows

After meeting with ICI, Herbosa said the commission encouraged DOH to keep digging and submit results.

DOH is rolling out a Citizens Participatory Audit so people can directly report idle facilities and irregularities—photos, dates, what's on the ground versus what's in the papers.

It's a simple idea: let the public help build the case file.

 Other Issues Shadowing the Health Chief

 Watchdogs Zero In

Baguio Mayor Benjie Magalong, who recently stepped away as an ICI adviser, warned that DOH is next on the reformers' radar.

He singled out mismanagement in medical assistance funds and urged ICI to widen its scope beyond public works.

Public health advocates echoed the call: follow the money, name the networks, and don't treat this as mere negligence.

 Congress Wants Answers

During budget deliberations, lawmakers asked a basic question: why are controls being "strengthened" only now, years after audit flags?

The comparison to DPWH is hard to avoid—paper projects, half-baked builds, and budget slicing that looks neat in spreadsheets but collapses in real life.

 What's Broken Under the Hood

The same pain points keep showing up: DOH and LGUs not coordinating on power, water, staffing, and opening; partial releases for multi-phase builds that strand projects midway; no clear playbook for turning a finished structure into a working clinic; and weak monitoring that lets idle buildings sit for years unnoticed.

 The Bigger Picture

This is happening while the larger infrastructure scandal continues to churn.

A key lawmaker resigned in the wake of the flood-control probe, and Senate hearings have paused "until further notice."

In that noise, the DOH saga cuts deeper because it hits healthcare capacity—right when the country still carries pandemic scars and a rising load of climate-related health risks.

 What to Watch Next

Expect three tracks: more site inspections and photo-verified reports from the public, stronger paper trails from DOH as it cleans up HFEP records, and possible case buildups once ICI vets the submissions.

Local chief executives are already budgeting to rescue stalled builds on their own, which tells you how urgent this is on the ground.

 What This Means for Us

If a clinic exists only on paper, people feel it in real life.

Mayor Benjamin “Benjie” Magalong’s public image as an anti-corruption crusader has earned him respect, but it’s also raised questions. 
His record is full of real battles against corruption, yet his silence on certain political figures leaves doubts about consistency and loyalty.
 His Record of Exposing Corruption
Magalong has shown genuine courage throughout his career. 
As a retired PNP official, he led the Board of Inquiry that investigated the 2015 Mamasapano massacre, which left 44 SAF commandos dead. 
His report named then-President Benigno Aquino III for violating the chain of command. 
Later, he went after PNP Chief Oscar Albayalde, presenting proof that Albayalde shielded officers involved in a 2013 drug recycling case.
In 2025, he again took center stage—this time exposing corruption in flood control projects, which he described as a “cottage industry” run by corrupt politicians, bureaucrats, and contractors. 
He even hand-delivered documents to President Marcos detailing irregularities in multi-billion peso infrastructure deals.
 Legal Battles and Ombudsman Clearances
As Baguio mayor, Magalong has faced multiple graft complaints. Yet the Ombudsman has repeatedly cleared him. 
In 2023, former City Councilor Mylen Yaranon accused him of pushing through a P95-million land purchase in Tuba, Benguet without proper approval. 
The Ombudsman dismissed the case for lack of probable cause. He was also cleared in another complaint tied to a DPWH-funded building project handled by a questionable contractor.
 The Credibility Problem: Duterte Ties and Selective Silence
This is where it gets uncomfortable. Magalong’s critics often point to his loyalty to the Dutertes and his reluctance to name names. 
Former Senator Antonio Trillanes once said Magalong told him who was behind the P36-billion rock-netting scam in Baguio and Benguet—but that was back in 2022. 
Three years later, the public still hasn’t heard those names.
Allegations suggest the people involved include Bong Go and Rep. Eric Yap, both known Duterte allies. 
Magalong has openly said he owes Bong Go a “great debt of gratitude” and still praises Duterte, describing him as “very genuine” and lauding his “transparency.” 
Whenever pressed to reveal those behind the scam, Magalong dodges the question.
VERA Files described him as “selective,” favoring investigations led by Duterte allies like Rodante Marcoleta, who conveniently skipped projects tied to Duterte’s administration. 
Their conclusion was sharp: “At his worst, he is not a truth-seeker because of his bias for Duterte and his tendency to obstruct justice.”
 ICI Resignation and the Conflict-of-Interest Issue
Magalong’s stint with the Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) lasted less than two weeks. 
He resigned on September 26, 2025, claiming that Palace statements had “undermined his mandate.” 
But the timing raised eyebrows. 
His exit came right after Malacañang looked into a possible conflict of interest—a P110-million tennis court and parking building in Baguio allegedly linked to the Discaya family, who were central to the flood control scandal he was investigating.
Rep. Terry Ridon didn’t mince words, calling Magalong “hypocritical” for keeping quiet about that link while condemning others for similar actions.
 Local Governance: Progress and Pushback
Since becoming Baguio mayor in 2019, Magalong has won re-election twice and co-founded the Mayors for Good Governance (M4GG) alliance with Pasig’s Vico Sotto and other reform-minded leaders. 
Still, locals have grown critical. 
Many complain about worsening traffic, weak infrastructure progress, and controversial development projects tied to the Villar family that led to deforestation.
Some residents also note that accessing documents from his office isn’t easy, accusing the city government of lacking transparency.
 His Past: The 2006 Coup Attempt
In 2006, Magalong was arrested for taking part in a coup attempt against then-President Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo. 
He spent three months in jail before being cleared of rebellion charges. 
That incident revealed a man willing to act against perceived abuse, even if it meant risking his career.
 The Verdict: Principled but Politically Bound
Deputy Speaker Ronaldo Puno, who once worked with Magalong at the DILG, described him as a “principled man” who wouldn’t accuse anyone of corruption without proof. 
The Tribune praised him for embodying the PMA values of “Courage, Integrity, Loyalty,” adding that no one has ever legally challenged his corruption exposés—suggesting there’s truth behind them.
Still, there’s a visible pattern. 
Magalong speaks firmly against corruption under the current administration but stays quiet on the Duterte-era cases he supposedly knows well. 
He calls for transparency while his own city office faces criticism for being opaque.
So, is he clean? 
Legally, yes—the Ombudsman says so. 
Personally, there’s no proof he’s pocketed public money. 
But politically, his silence weakens his credibility. 
He preaches accountability yet avoids confronting his political patrons. That silence speaks volumes.
Magalong doesn’t fit neatly into any box. 
He’s neither the spotless reformer some believe him to be, nor a traditional crook. 
He’s a man shaped by conviction, limited by alliances, and caught in the gray area of Philippine politics—where principle and politics rarely stay separate for long.

In case you missed it, the flood control scandal that has rocked the Department of Public Works and Highways (DPWH) continues to grow. 

The Independent Commission for Infrastructure (ICI) has confirmed 421 suspected ghost projects out of roughly 8,000 reviewed. 

These are projects that existed on paper but not on the ground—no construction, no materials, no beneficiaries.

ICI Executive Director Brian Keith Hosaka said the commission is preparing to file 15 to 20 new cases within the next few weeks. 

What began as a standard audit of flood control projects has now turned into a coordinated national probe involving the AFP, PNP, and NBI to validate sites and trace how public funds disappeared.

The ICI’s work has also shifted toward asset recovery. 

The Anti-Money Laundering Council has already frozen around ₱5 billion across nearly 3,000 bank accounts tied to individuals and companies involved. 

ICI Chairman Andres Reyes Jr. stressed that prosecution alone isn’t enough—the goal is restitution. 

The government wants the money back in public coffers, not just names in court records.

To make this happen, a multi-agency task force now includes the Ombudsman, DOJ, Solicitor General, DPWH, and AMLC. 

Their focus: speed up both criminal cases and civil forfeitures.

Still, the investigation hit a snag when contractor couple Pacifico “Curlee” and Cezarah “Sarah” Discaya decided to withdraw cooperation.

Their companies were awarded more than ₱31 billion in flood control projects, and their earlier testimonies had implicated several lawmakers and DPWH officials. 

They’re now invoking their right against self-incrimination—a move that could slow down the gathering of direct evidence.

Over in the Senate, the leadership shuffle continues. Senator Erwin Tulfo is now acting chair of the Blue Ribbon Committee after Senator Panfilo Lacson resigned. 

Tulfo said the committee plans to hold two final hearings in November before closing the flood control probe and shifting to DPWH’s farm-to-market road projects, which are reportedly overpriced by another ₱10 billion.

Inside DPWH, the fallout has reached the upper ranks. 

Undersecretary Arrey Perez resigned on October 17 after being accused of having ties with contractors. 

Secretary Vince Dizon accepted his resignation and vowed to “cleanse the department from top to bottom.” 

An internal Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Committee has since been formed to investigate further.

 Who Has Been Interviewed So Far

The ICI’s investigation hasn’t just been about documents—it’s been face-to-face. The commission has called in cabinet members, senators, and former DPWH officials to testify about how the money moved and who signed off on the projects.

Among those interviewed were former DPWH Secretaries Manuel Bonoan and Mark Villar, both questioned about projects under their watch. 

Senators Jinggoy Estrada, Francis “Chiz” Escudero, and Joel Villanueva have also faced the commission after being tagged by DPWH engineers in connection with kickback schemes.

High-profile political figures have likewise appeared, including former Speaker Martin Romualdez, who attended hearings in October and is scheduled for another round this month. 

Former Ako Bicol Rep. Zaldy Co, meanwhile, remains abroad despite repeated subpoenas and now faces possible contempt charges.

On the contractors’ side, Pacifico “Curlee” and Cezarah “Sarah” Discaya initially cooperated before invoking their right against self-incrimination. 

Their testimony had named several officials and lawmakers before they backed out. 

The ICI has also questioned former DPWH assistant engineers Brice Hernandez and Jaypee Mendoza, both under tight security, with Hernandez reportedly surrendering his luxury vehicle to investigators.

Budget Secretary Amenah Pangandaman has been asked to appear in upcoming sessions, and ICI officials say more DPWH personnel are being lined up for questioning. 

Even the US Ambassador paid a visit to the ICI office to express interest in the ongoing probe—proof that the scandal has drawn international attention.

Meanwhile, the ICI has summoned former Speaker Martin Romualdez for questioning and continues to chase former Ako Bicol Rep. Zaldy Co, who has yet to return from Spain despite multiple subpoenas. 

Investigators are also checking possible links between the Discaya companies and CLTG Builders, a firm allegedly connected to Senator Bong Go’s family—a claim Go has publicly denied.

Beyond the politics, this scandal has already shaken business confidence and deepened public anger. 

Billions meant for flood control—funds that could have spared lives and homes—were instead siphoned off into ghost projects. 

For a country battered by typhoons year after year, it’s more than theft. It’s neglect.

As the ICI deepens its investigation, one truth keeps surfacing: this isn’t just about failed projects. It exposes how disaster funds were treated like personal cash. 

The next few weeks—especially once those 15 to 20 cases are filed—will show if justice can finally cut through the mud.

When federal prosecutors in Florida added Smartmatic and its parent SGO Corporation to the criminal indictment on October 16, 2025, they alleged a decade-long scheme that treated elections like a product you could buy with briefcases of cash.

### The Price of Democracy: P180 Billion in Contracts and a Million-Dollar Kickback

Between 2015 and 2018, Smartmatic executives allegedly moved at least $1 million to Juan Andres Bautista, then COMELEC chair. In exchange, they secured more than $180 million in contracts to supply 90,000 voting machines for the 2016 elections. That election reshaped Philippine politics for years.

The mechanics were brazen. Prices for each machine were bumped by $10 to $50 to build a slush fund. Coded language covered the trail, along with fabricated contracts and loan agreements. The money moved through accounts across Asia, Europe, and the U.S., including Florida.

Five days after Bautista approved dropping VAT from 12% to 5% and cleared over $4 million in payments to Smartmatic, executives Roger Pinate and Jorge Vasquez initiated $1 million in transfers while in Florida. The timing speaks for itself.

### The Man Who Fled: Juan Andres Bautista’s Fall From Grace

Bautista served as COMELEC chair from April 2015 to October 2017, overseeing one of our most consequential elections. Prosecutors say the bribe money helped purchase a two-bedroom condo in San Francisco’s Pacific Heights.

His downfall started at home. In August 2017, his estranged wife Patricia alleged he held more than 30 bank accounts with unexplained wealth not declared in his SALN and that he received commissions via DivinaLaw, Smartmatic’s local counsel, for “assisting the law firm’s clients with the Comelec.”

He was impeached 137-75-2 on October 11, 2017, hours after announcing his resignation. On November 21, 2017, he flew to the United States and never returned despite a Senate subpoena. He now faces money laundering charges in both Philippine and U.S. courts, a walking example of how the powerful often sidestep accountability.

### The Questions We Still Avoid

What does a $1 million bribe say about the integrity of the 2016 procurement? COMELEC itself admitted in 2023 that the raps cast a heavy shadow on protocols and public confidence. That’s putting it mildly.

Smartmatic handled the 2010, 2013, 2016, and 2022 elections here, with at least ₱25 billion in total contracts. If one process was tainted, who can say with certainty the others were clean? We’ve heard the complaints for years—machine malfunctions, suspicious glitches, transmission issues, and bidding rules bent to favor a single vendor.

A 2022 technical study out of Royal Holloway, University of London, examined the 2016 automated election and found the system failed 5 of 8 trust-model properties. Transparency—especially around certification and source code review—was the weak point. They looked at 426 log files from 192 clustered precincts and reached one conclusion: lack of transparency kills trust.

Yet by June 2019, surveys showed 84% of voters had “big trust” in the automated process. That gap between technical reality and public perception is a vulnerability. We trusted a system we didn’t fully understand, run by a company now accused of bribing the very officials in charge of oversight.

### The Political Timing Problem

The first indictment against individual executives landed in August 2024 under Biden’s DOJ. The move to add Smartmatic as a corporate defendant came in October 2025 under Trump’s DOJ.

Complication: on February 10, 2025, the White House issued an order pausing FCPA enforcement for 180 days for a review of all ongoing cases. Even so, on April 9, 2025, the DOJ filed a notice saying it had reviewed the case and would proceed to trial. No details on the review, no explanation of the decision-making.

Smartmatic says powerful interests influenced the case despite its cooperation. Meanwhile, the company’s $2.7 billion defamation suit against Fox News is still in play. Fox points to the indictments and claims Smartmatic’s reputation was dogged by failure and controversy long before 2020. The irony writes itself: a firm suing over election lies is fighting criminal charges tied to a foreign election scheme. Whether this represents justice or revenge depends on who you ask, but it adds another problem for the rule of law.

### From Smartmatic to Miru: Did We Fix Anything?

COMELEC disqualified Smartmatic from future bids in November 2023 to protect election integrity. The Supreme Court reversed this with finality in November 2024, but the ship had sailed—COMELEC had already awarded a ₱17.9 billion contract to Miru Systems for 2025.

Miru’s record doesn’t inspire confidence. From 2015 to 2017, it allegedly landed deals in Fiji, Ecuador, and El Salvador through a South Korean ODA arrangement worth about $4.4 million, granted directly without open bidding. Local groups here flagged failures in Congo and Iraq where Miru was involved; the company denied those claims.

In December 2023, Miru was initially disqualified over documentation issues. When bidding reopened, six other firms signaled interest but only Miru actually bid. A supposed competition with one runner. By October 2024, their local partner, St. Timothy Construction Corporation, withdrew due to potential conflicts of interest as one of its owners was running in 2025.

That ₱17.9 billion deal became one of the most expensive election procurements we’ve had, awarded through a process many critics said made a joke of procurement rules. Some lawmakers urged COMELEC to reuse the existing Smartmatic machines to save billions. COMELEC refused, citing past glitches.

We moved away from a provider accused of corruption toward one accused of incompetence. Progress, apparently.

### The Larger Crisis: Why Corruption Survives—and What It Costs

The Smartmatic mess is a reflection of how corruption has become routine in public life. In many developing democracies, weak enforcement means re-election incentives are expected to deter graft, but that approach fails when patronage systems reward loyalty over integrity.

Evidence from places like Afghanistan and Uganda shows monitoring technology can cut fraud by 60% and improve turnout by 25%, but only if leaders actually want those safeguards. Here, we’ve rarely demanded what builds trust: open code, independent audits, real accountability.

As early as 2010, the National Democratic Institute observed that suspicion between rivals, distrust of COMELEC, and lack of transparency corroded public confidence in our elections. Fifteen years later, the diagnosis still fits because we never fixed the culture that lets corruption breathe.

When people stop believing the count, they stop believing the system. When powerful officials can leave the country instead of facing charges, the lesson is ugly: the law is for those without protection. And when big contracts go to sole bidders with shaky track records, “reform” turns into an expensive costume.

### The Path Forward: Accountability or Impunity—Pick One

The U.S. case is heading toward trial. The executives face serious time—up to 25 years on the money laundering counts. For Smartmatic, a conviction could be existential: fines that match the $180 million benefit, debarment from contracts, and reputational collapse.

What about here at home? Bautista is still out of reach. No other local officials have been charged. A bribery scheme like this needs more than one pair of hands. There’s no comprehensive audit of 2016 to see whether bribes influenced results. Cases at the Ombudsman linger without a clear finish line.

We punish the headline villain and ignore the system that made the crime possible. We blacklist one vendor and use the same broken process for the next deal. We rage at bribes but barely touch how ₱25 billion in contracts were awarded while transparency stayed weak.

This should be a turning point. It can be a demand for real reform—procurement with teeth, audits with consequences, and accountability that doesn’t care about last names. My worry: it fades like the scandals before it, while the machine keeps humming.

### A Warning for 2025 and Beyond

With Miru running 2025, every voter should be asking uncomfortable questions. What safeguards exist to prevent a new bribery playbook? How do we verify results beyond press releases? What happens if machines fail or data looks wrong?

We’ve spent ₱25 billion over 15 years on automation and can’t honestly say our elections are fully transparent or verifiable. We favored speed and convenience over proofs that ordinary people can check. We accepted technical excuses instead of demanding independent tests. We let procurement drift into a world where citizens have less and less say.

This case shows what happens when oversight is weak and consequences are light. Vendors will pay. Officials will take. And the cycle keeps turning unless people insist on rules that actually bite.

Democracy isn’t just lining up and tapping a screen. It’s knowing the count is honest and the report is true. Without a system that can promise that, everything built on top of it sits on sand.

Smartmatic has given us another opening to fix the basics. Either we push for accountability and reform, or we just change the logos and hope for the best while deals happen in the dark.

For the sake of our vote, I’m choosing accountability. History says this country often doesn’t. That’s why you’re here with me.

We keep talking about transparency—something that should be done but, for many, is difficult to do.
There are a handful of LGUs already taking this seriously. But if I were president, here’s how I’d do it.
I’d turn the Philippines into a glass-walled republic. A country where government has no corners hidden from public view, where every peso spent, every meeting held, and every decision made is open to the eyes of the people who pay for it.
Corruption thrives in silence.
When everything happens behind closed doors, trust dies a quiet death. If we want to rebuild that trust, government must be visible, not just accountable.

 The Glass Walls We Need
Every office—from City Halls to the LTO, from the BIR to the Office of the President—should have mandatory live streams in their main service areas.
Cameras should show how long queues really take, how documents are processed, and whether public servants are doing their jobs with integrity.
The public deserves to see what their taxes are funding, not through edited PR clips but through unfiltered daily reality.
Imagine walking into your local government office and seeing everyone behind transparent glass walls.
No fixers. No backroom deals.
The entire transaction floor visible to the public.
People could witness justice, licensing, and governance happening in broad daylight.
Private rooms would still exist for medical or legal confidentiality, but logs showing who entered and exited should be visible to all.
Transparency shouldn’t just be a value—it should be a physical design principle. A government literally built in glass.
Imagine the comfort of knowing that when you line up for a driver’s license, the process is being recorded and broadcast.
That when someone files a permit, the transaction can be seen by anyone.
Even meetings between officials, bidding sessions, and agency consultations should be livestreamed and archived, unless they involve legitimate matters of national security.

 Technology as the Great Equalizer
Technology can make this possible. The Department of Budget and Management already moved toward digital governance. The tools exist—but they’re often too technical, hidden behind systems only experts can understand. People don’t really see where their money goes; they only notice when projects are delayed, overpriced, or unfinished.
What we need now is something simpler, more visible—an Open Government Cloud. One place where people can see real-time spending, check project status, view signed contracts, and even watch the work happen live. A platform where national and local transparency finally meet.

 Transparency in Law and Practice
The 2024 Government Procurement Act is a good start. It forces contractors to reveal their real owners, ending the use of shell companies. But that’s just one layer.
Every bidding process—from the start of the meeting to the final awarding—should be livestreamed, archived, and open to the public.
The People’s Budget Portal should also evolve into something interactive. Imagine a live dashboard that updates every time funds are released, transferred, or spent.
No more hidden budgets or unexplained increases.
Even confidential funds could appear as anonymized totals, so at least the public knows they exist and how much they cost.

 The People’s Right to Watch
Picture a map of the Philippines online. Each province a small glowing dot.
Click one, and you can see its government offices in real time.
You’d know if the LTO is crowded or if your City Hall is open and serving properly.
You could even trace how national funds travel from Congress to DBM to the contractor’s account—timestamped, transparent, and undeniable.
And it shouldn’t stop there.
Journalists, civic groups, even vloggers could act as independent watchers.
Every document, memo, and receipt should be digitally filed and accessible for public review. No selective transparency.
No token disclosures. Just open governance as a habit.

 Sunlight as Deterrent
A system like this would make corruption hard to hide. When everyone can watch, everyone behaves differently.
Transparency won’t make people perfect, but it makes wrongdoing harder.
When sunlight enters the room, mold doesn’t grow.
Of course, there will be challenges—privacy, data protection, slow internet—but those can be fixed.
The real issue is political will.
Because transparency is easy to promise, but terrifying to those who’ve built careers in the dark.

 Visible Governance as a National Standard
If we ever manage to build a culture of visible governance, it could change how this country functions.
Transparency wouldn’t just be a law—it would be part of daily life.
Every peso, every permit, every project done in daylight.
That’s the kind of Republic I dream of.
A nation built on glass walls, where honesty isn’t heroic—it’s expected.
And maybe one day, when that happens, we’ll finally see who’s really working—and who’s just posing for the camera.

 How This Would Affect Bureaucracy
A glass-walled government would shake the bureaucracy from top to bottom. It would expose inefficiencies, delays, and the small shortcuts that everyone pretends don’t exist.
For some, that would be uncomfortable. For others, liberating.
Government offices have always lived on paperwork, hierarchy, and habit. Suddenly, those habits would be under constant public view. Every delay traceable.
Every transaction recorded. Some workers would feel pressured, others would finally feel protected—because visibility cuts both ways.
It exposes misconduct, but it also shields those doing the right thing.
For older bureaucrats used to discretion, this would feel like losing control.
For younger, tech-savvy employees, it might feel like justice finally catching up.
But cultural shifts take time. We can’t expect decades of secrecy to dissolve overnight.

 Would Lawmakers Vote Against It?
That’s where it gets tricky. Lawmakers love the idea of transparency—until it reaches their offices.
If a Glass-Wall Transparency Act ever made it to Congress, expect the usual performance.
Speeches about accountability, handshakes for the cameras, promises of reform.
Then the quiet amendments would come in: exemptions for committees, exclusions for “sensitive” meetings, clauses that keep power where it’s always been.
Still, every small win matters.
Even if some sessions stay off-camera, the habit of openness spreads once it starts. Civil society won’t stop watching.
Because once people taste transparency, they don’t want to go back to blind trust.

 The Likely Scenario
It won’t be perfect. Some will resist.
Others will try to outsmart the system.
But the point isn’t to create a flawless government—it’s to make dishonesty harder to hide.
Every camera installed, every document uploaded, every live feed opened chips away at the old culture of secrecy.
Transparency doesn’t destroy bureaucracy—it forces it to grow up.

 The Hard Truth
I know this sounds idealistic.
With how deeply corruption runs in this country, it feels almost impossible.
And I’m not just talking about the government—we’re a corrupt nation in spirit.
From fixers in offices to under-the-table deals in small communities, corruption has become habit.
It’s part of how people “get things done.”
That’s what makes this vision feel like a fantasy.
A glass-walled republic would threaten not just politicians, but a mindset that’s been passed down for generations.
The kind that says, “Wala namang mawawala kung makiusap.”
If this ever happens, it shouldn’t just depend on one president’s will.
Transparency shouldn’t be a campaign promise—it should be a law.
Something permanent, that can be passed from one administration to the next.
Systems can change, leaders can change, but visibility should stay.

 A Quiet Thought Over Coffee
Maybe that’s what scares them most—the thought of being seen.
Not by the press, not by their peers, but by ordinary people who have nothing to lose and everything to gain from honesty.
Transparency won’t fix everything, but it changes the rhythm of power.
It reminds those who lead that visibility isn’t punishment; it’s a mirror.
And sometimes, the hardest thing about seeing yourself clearly is realizing how much you’ve hidden.
If one day we wake up to a government built on glass, it won’t just be the officials who are visible.
It’ll be us too—finally aware, finally watching, and finally part of the light.

 Is This the Same as Glasnost?
Some might ask if this sounds like glasnost—that Soviet idea of openness under Mikhail Gorbachev during the mid-1980s.
In spirit, yes. But in form, not really.
Glasnost was political, born out of dictatorship.
It gave people permission to speak after decades of silence.
My version doesn’t need permission—it needs infrastructure.
It’s not about loosening control but designing visibility so that honesty becomes routine.
Gorbachev’s glasnost relied on leadership willing to listen.
A glass-walled republic relies on systems that can’t lie.
One was a reform; the other could be a structure.
Both spring from the same truth: secrecy poisons everything.
But unlike glasnost, which vanished with its leader, a glass-walled government could outlive politics.
It would exist in code, cameras, and data—less about ideology, more about habit.
Maybe that’s our own kind of glasnost. Not a revolution, just quiet honesty.
Not an uprising, but a daily reminder that power should never fear the light.

I sometimes look east and see the Sierra Madre still wrapped in morning mist.
It looks whole from here. But those who live near its slopes hear another sound—the growl of engines, the scrape of rock, the steady line of trucks taking the mountain apart one load at a time.
We keep calling it progress.
Roads that shorten travel. Concrete for homes. Bridges for trade.
Every project begins with a cut on the land that shelters us.
The mountain doesn’t complain. It loosens quietly.
Rain turns the soil to slurry.
And one day, everything carved too deep gives way.
Officials take the first blame, and they should.
But the truth runs wider.
We keep buying the materials. We drive the roads.
The dust is on everyone’s hands.

 The Death Toll of Progress
In 2018, the town of Naga in Cebu woke to a sound like thunder.
A slope—hollowed by years of quarrying by Apo Land and Quarry Corporation—collapsed after heavy rain.
Seventy-eight people were confirmed dead; five were never found.
Months earlier, thirty-five miners in Itogon, Benguet, were buried alive.
In February 2024, a landslide in a Mindanao mining town killed almost a hundred and displaced more than 7,000.
That October, during Typhoon Kristine, fourteen residents of Talisay, Batangas, were buried under mud traced to illegal quarrying.
When Typhoon Ulysses hit in 2020, the Marikina River swelled to 22 meters—higher than Ondoy.
Scientists linked the flooding to deforestation and quarrying in the Upper Marikina Watershed.
In Rodriguez, Rizal—known locally as Montalban—the quarrying has carved deep into the Sierra Madre, stripping forests and leaving communities vulnerable to floods each rainy season.
By 2024, when Typhoon Carina struck, torrents triggered by the stripped slopes of Rizal forced 31,000 Marikina residents to evacuate—proof that four years after Ulysses, the same wounds were left open.
These disasters keep repeating.
We still call them natural.
They aren’t.

 The Wounds Left Behind
Quarrying leaves scars you can measure.
In General Santos, studies showed spikes in dust, soil erosion, and water contamination.
Along the Davao River, quarry sites turned the water brown; researchers warned the turbidity may never clear.
The damage spreads far past the pits.
Bare slopes feed floods downstream.
Eroded soil clogs rivers and buries farmland.
In Bicol, 500 hectares across seven towns are now open pits.
Natural embankments are gone; floods reach higher each year.
At Mount Banahaw, illegal blasts shake windows, and the Lagnas River keeps shrinking.
The land remembers every blow. It always does.

 Our Complicity
The construction-aggregates market was valued at ₱3.97 billion in 2025 and is projected to hit ₱5.85 billion by 2032—a 5.7 percent growth rate fed by demand.
Build Build Build created that demand.
Projects such as the Metro Manila Subway, the North–South Commuter Railway, and a chain of new expressways require more than 10,000 metric tons of sand and gravel every day.
From 2020 to 2024, construction grew 11.8 percent a year, while infrastructure spending rose from 4.2 to 5.8 percent of GDP.
In 2022, quarrying for stone, sand, and clay made up 68.6 percent of all mining sites.
By 2023 the industry employed 210,174 people, its highest since 2020.
The wider construction sector now employs over 5 million workers and contributes 7.7 percent of GDP.
These are wages that feed families.
But they’re built on ground that can no longer breathe.
We buy the houses, drive the roads, work inside the towers.
Every convenience is tied to a hole somewhere in this country.

 Corruption and Impunity
Permits are meant to protect the environment; instead they’re traded like currency.
The Philippine Institute for Development Studies noted that multiple mining agencies create “corridors for corruption.”
In Cebu, Governor Gwendolyn Garcia sought to void Apo Land’s Mineral Production Sharing Agreement after finding millions of tons extracted with no provincial revenue since 2008.
Operations still continued. Later she was suspended for alleged irregular permits.
In Nueva Ecija, Governor Aurelio Umali was suspended in 2025 over 205 quarrying permits.
In Oriental Mindoro, Governor Bonz Dolor accused contractors of operating illegally and avoiding taxes.
In Bulacan, Governor Daniel Fernando suspended mining in 2022; a month later, five rescuers died during Super Typhoon Karding, floods worsened by stripped hillsides.
The Commission on Human Rights calls this what it is: a culture of impunity.
Reports fade; the ground keeps falling.

 The Manila Bay Experiment
Out on Manila Bay, the scale of destruction is larger.
Ten seabed-quarry projects have been approved to supply materials for reclamation.
The Philippine Reclamation Authority has cleared 13 of 25 applications—5,000 hectares of artificial land, about the size of Caloocan City, rising from the water.
Fisher groups Pamalakaya and Kalikasan say these operations destroy coral reefs, raise turbidity, and rob 300,000 fisherfolk of their livelihood.
Marine ecosystems collapse, flood risks rise, yet the dredgers work through the night.
We’ll soon stroll through malls built where fishing boats once docked.
When the next surge hits, we’ll call it a tragedy.

 The False Choice
We keep pretending we must choose between growth and protection.
That’s never been true.
The construction industry employs 5 million Filipinos; Build Build Build promised 1.1 million jobs yearly.
But NEDA itself warns that unregulated quarrying endangers the same future those jobs are meant to build.
Senator Risa Hontiveros called for inquiry, saying, “The devastation and loss caused by mining and quarrying are distressing. Not only livelihoods are lost but also lives.”
Studies from PIDS echo it: environmental losses outweigh economic gains.
We keep calling it development. The land calls it exhaustion.

 Breaking the Cycle
Groups such as Tanggol Kalikasan, Kalikasan People’s Network, Greenpeace Philippines, and Haribon Foundation keep fighting for what’s left.
Indigenous Dumagat-Remontado communities guard the Sierra Madre with little protection from the state.
The risk is lethal.
Between 2012 and 2018, Global Witness recorded 178 environmental defenders killed in the Philippines, the deadliest record worldwide.
Still, many try.
A Standard Insights survey found 91.8 percent of Filipinos believe individual action can change the planet’s future.
Two-thirds conserve energy or reduce waste.
More than 57 percent are willing to pay extra for sustainable goods.
Belief must turn into pressure.
Enforce mining laws. Cancel permits in protected zones.
Publish collections and audits. Rehabilitate exhausted pits.
Protect defenders. Halt seabed quarrying until it’s proven safe.
Anything less is surrender.

 The Mirror We Must Face
Quarrying reflects who we are: people who demand houses and roads faster than the land can recover.
In the Upper Marikina River Basin, only 11.7 percent of forest remains.
Twenty quarrying firms still operate there with state approval.
When Typhoon Carina struck in 2024, floods reached even Antipolo and Montalban.
Local groups like Gabriela Women’s Party–Marikina said it clearly: local cleanup means nothing if national leaders keep approving destructive projects.
The blame can’t stop at government desks.
It sits with every purchase and every silence that lets the digging continue.
Bodies buried in landslides. Families driven out by floods.
Fisherfolk losing their boats.
Activists killed for defending what’s left.
This is the price of our convenience.
Until we admit that, the mountains will keep falling and we’ll keep counting the dead while building over their graves.

There’s something about Cabanatuan that feels both familiar and complicated to me.
The city hums with the sound of tricycles — engines rattling down every street, lines forming near terminals, and passengers negotiating fares as if it were part of daily prayer.
But underneath that routine is a story that stretches beyond transportation. The story runs deeper — survival, control, and the politics that keep the wheels turning.
When I began looking into Cabanatuan’s tricycle industry, I wasn’t just studying transport data or city ordinances.
I was seeing how an entire economy breathes — how one policy can ripple through thousands of families, and how every attempt at reform collides with something deeper: fear, habit, and power.
Cabanatuan calls itself the Tricycle Capital of the Philippines, and it’s not hard to see why.
Yet behind that pride lies a question that’s hard to ignore: how long can a city built on three wheels keep moving forward?

 The Reality on the Road
In Cabanatuan, the tricycle is both a mode of transportation and a livelihood. It’s a small economy, and sometimes, the only way to get anywhere.
But ask any commuter and you’ll hear the same complaint: fare abuse.
Officially, fares are simple — ₱8 per kilometer for students, ₱10 for regular passengers, and ₱7 for persons with disabilities.
In practice, those numbers barely exist.
A ₱14 fare easily turns into ₱20 or ₱30, and a five-kilometer trip that should cost ₱22 can reach ₱60 or ₱70.
It’s been going on for years.
Even a petition in 2019 demanding fair pricing didn’t change much.
Students, who make up most of the city’s passengers, continue to bear the weight of those inflated fares.
City Ordinance 2007-040 was supposed to stop this, imposing penalties for overcharging.
But enforcement rarely followed.
It’s not that the rules are unclear.
It’s more of, no one wants to challenge a system that feeds thousands of families — especially when elections are always around the corner.

 When Policy Meets Poverty
To ease traffic congestion, the city tried a color-coding scheme.
Each tricycle was assigned a color, allowed to operate only on certain days.
On paper, it sounded organized.
In reality, it became another burden.
Drivers who rent their tricycles for ₱100 to ₱150 a day suddenly lost income when they couldn’t operate daily.
For many, every missed trip meant less food on the table.
The program may have reduced congestion, but it also showed how policy designed from city hall often misses what happens on the street.
The result was predictable — frustration, noncompliance, and a quiet resentment toward any reform that takes away earning time.
Urban planning is a delicate thing.
In Cabanatuan, it often becomes a balancing act between order and survival, and survival almost always wins.

 Technology, Hope, and Hesitation
Then came the idea of electronic fare meters — small devices meant to settle fare disputes once and for all.
In theory, it should have been simple: standard fares, fair charges, and no more haggling.
But the reception was mixed.
Drivers saw the potential for transparency and trust, but they also saw the cost.
Installing meters means expenses many can’t afford, plus the fear of earning less once fares become fixed.
A study in 2025 captured that contradiction perfectly: drivers want fairness, but not at the cost of feeding their families.
The irony is painful.
The very people who could benefit from reform are the ones most afraid of it — not because they reject progress, but because progress, in their experience, often comes at their expense.

 Grab Tricycle and the Digital Experiment
When Mayor Myca Vergara opened talks with Grab Philippines to introduce Grab Tricycle services, it felt like the city was finally stepping into the digital age.
Fixed fares, 24/7 service, better transparency — everything passengers had been asking for.
But modernization in a city like Cabanatuan isn’t just about apps.
The real challenge is inclusion.
Most tricycle drivers don’t own smartphones, and many are wary of apps that might replace them.
Grab promises efficiency, but if not managed carefully, it could divide the sector — those who can adapt and those who get left behind.
Still, it’s a step forward.
The question is whether city leadership has the courage to follow through once the political noise begins — because every reform that threatens an old system tends to die quietly once campaign season looms.

 The Unseen Weight of Weak Enforcement
Cabanatuan has rules for everything: fares, color coding, licensing, even environmental compliance.
What it doesn’t have is consistent enforcement.
Thousands of tricycles operate without permits.
Estimates place around 6,000 colorum units alongside 13,966 registered ones.
That’s almost half the fleet running beyond regulation — clogging traffic, competing unfairly, and eroding trust in the system.
Yet violations rarely lead to penalties.
Ask why, and you’ll often hear the same answer whispered: politics.
The city can’t afford to anger thousands of drivers, not when each one has a family who votes.
It’s easier to look the other way than to risk the backlash.
That’s how weak political will becomes policy.

 The Struggle to Survive
Behind the steering wheels are stories of exhaustion.
The average tricycle driver earns between ₱193 and ₱400 a day — barely enough to keep up with fuel prices and household needs.
Most are breadwinners with no other source of income.
Some have debts; others rent their tricycles and owe part of their daily income to operators.
With that kind of pressure, fare overcharging becomes greed and survival instinct.
The oversupply of tricycles, paired with low passenger demand, creates cutthroat competition.
Every peso becomes a fight to stay afloat.
For many, driving a tricycle isn’t a career anymore. It’s the only option they have on the table.

 Planning a City Around Tricycles
Cabanatuan’s dependence on tricycles defines its urban identity.
More than half of the city’s commuters rely on them every day.
That level of dependence makes regulation tricky.
A national directive once banned tricycles from national highways, including Maharlika Highway, to reduce accidents and traffic.
But Cabanatuan couldn’t comply without paralyzing mobility.
Tricycles fill the gap where buses and jeepneys don’t go. Remove them too quickly, and the entire local economy stalls.
The city sits at a crossroads — literally and politically.
Should it continue to build around tricycles or start phasing them out for modern alternatives?
Every choice carries consequences.
Every delay buys political safety.

 The Power of Numbers
Across the country, there are roughly 1.5 million registered tricycles and around 4 million drivers.
In Cabanatuan alone, over 30,000 operate within city limits.
Add their families, and they easily make up 15 to 20 percent of the electorate.
That’s power and statistics politicians know.
During elections, the Tricycle Operators and Drivers Associations — TODAs — become ready-made political machines.
They organize quickly, reach neighborhoods that campaign teams can’t, and can shift votes in a city where margins are thin.
Officials don’t stall because they’re confused. They stall because every crackdown risks losing votes.

 TODAs and the Business of Politics
Cabanatuan has 57 registered TODAs, each with its own network of members, operators, and local alliances.
For politicians, that’s an irresistible setup: organized, influential, and reachable through a few key leaders.
Over the years, this relationship has blurred lines between public service and patronage.
Cash assistance or “fuel subsidies” distributed near election season often come with subtle reminders of who to thank.
Party-list groups claiming to represent tricycle drivers — some even led by wealthy families — reinforce the illusion of representation while maintaining control.
In short, the tricycle sector doesn’t just transport people. It moves votes.
The people who gain the most from this setup aren’t the drivers. They’re the politicians who know exactly how to use them come election season.

 When Fear Dictates Policy
In 2011, an ordinance sought to ban 5,000 non-resident tricycles from operating in Cabanatuan.
On paper, it aimed to reduce congestion and regulate outsiders.
In reality, it was softened into a “rationalization” plan — a quiet retreat dressed as compromise.
The reason? Fear of public backlash.
In 2019, when the DILG directed cities to keep tricycles off Maharlika Highway, local officials negotiated for delays, claiming lack of alternate routes.
It was the same story: no one wanted to face the political cost of compliance.
Even the transition to electric tricycles — promoted as a step toward sustainability — has been repeatedly postponed.
Officials cite “livelihood concerns” and “economic adjustment,” but the truth is simpler: reform is risky when your next election depends on those you’re trying to regulate.

 When Drivers Push Back
Tricycle drivers aren’t passive players in this story. They’ve learned to organize when pushed too far.
In Dumaguete, 2,800 drivers protested against a massive reclamation project, arguing it would hurt local livelihoods.
During the pandemic, drivers launched “Tigil Pasada” strikes to ease passenger restrictions — and won concessions the same day.
These actions showed how local governments often bend under collective pressure.
The same pattern exists in Cabanatuan, though quieter.
When regulations tighten, TODA leaders meet with city hall, negotiations follow, and enforcement relaxes.
It’s political choreography: push, resist, and settle somewhere in between.

 The Illusion of Empowerment
In early 2025, Congress unanimously passed the Magna Carta for Tricycle Drivers and Operators — a long-overdue recognition of their rights and welfare.
It was praised as progress, with promises of SSS and PhilHealth benefits.
Senators and congressmen across parties proudly attached their names to it.
But even that legislation mirrors the same dynamic that created the problem.
It recognizes the tricycle sector not because of its economic contribution alone, but because of its political weight.
A unanimous vote rarely comes from conviction. It comes from self-preservation.
It’s easier to pass a law that flatters a powerful sector than to fix the messy realities of enforcement and reform.

 Power, Captured
Despite their numbers, tricycle drivers still operate within a system dominated by elite politics.
Families who have ruled cities for decades continue to shape how modernization happens — or doesn’t.
In Cabanatuan, the Vergara family has managed this balance for years.
Under their administration, modernization talks continue, but enforcement remains soft.
Grab Tricycle negotiations move forward, but timelines remain vague.
Illegal tricycles still roam freely.
Every policy ends where convenience begins.
What looks like incompetence is often self-preservation — from city hall down to the people who depend on it.

 Moving Forward, Carefully
The city’s tricycle problem isn’t just a transportation issue.
The real issue lies in how power operates when livelihood and politics can’t be separated.
Regulations exist, but they’re rarely followed.
Modernization is promised, but often postponed.
Everyone knows the system is broken, but no one wants to be the one blamed for fixing it.
Real reform in Cabanatuan requires courage — the kind that values long-term development over short-term votes.
It means standing up to the pattern of appeasement that has defined local governance for years.
It means helping drivers transition into better livelihoods instead of trapping them in the same cycle of debt and dependency.
But courage is expensive in politics.
And for now, silence costs less.

 My Takeaway
Every time I ride the tricycle around Cabanatuan, I’m reminded of how a city’s heartbeat can tell you everything about its politics.
The sound of tricycles says everything. It speaks of people trying to get through another day and leaders who’ve learned to live with delay — waiting out problems instead of solving them.
Still, I can’t dismiss the drivers.
They are the city’s soul, enduring the heat, the debt, and the long days just to feed their families.
But I also can’t ignore how fear — both theirs and the politicians’ — keeps Cabanatuan in a loop of half-measures.
Someday, someone will have to decide between convenience and courage.
Between another term in office and genuine change.
Until then, the engines will keep roaring, the fares will keep rising, and the city that calls itself the Tricycle Capital will keep moving — but never far enough.
